channel.title: Typescript on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions
channel.description: Recent content in Typescript on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript Cannot Find Module — Module Resolution and Type Declarations Fix
item.description:
TypeScript: Cannot find module ‘X’ or its type declarations

This error occurs when TypeScript cannot resolve a module import. It happens when the module doesn’t exist, hasn’t been installed, is missing type declarations, or when tsconfig.json module resolution settings don’t match the project structure.

Common Causes

  • Module not installed — node_modules is missing the package
  • Missing @types package — JavaScript packages need separate type declarations
  • Incorrect moduleResolution in tsconfig — using "node" when the package needs "node16" or "bundler"
  • Path aliases not configured — importing via aliases without paths in tsconfig

How to Fix

# Fix 1: Install the missing package
npm install <package-name>

# Fix 2: Install type declarations
npm install --save-dev @types/<package-name>

# Fix 3: Some packages include their own types — no @types needed
npm install express # express includes types

// Fix 4: Configure tsconfig.json for module resolution
{
 "compilerOptions": {
 "moduleResolution": "node",
 "baseUrl": ".",
 "paths": {
 "@/*": ["./src/*"]
 }
 }
}

// Fix 5: Create a declaration file for untyped modules
// src/types/my-module.d.ts
declare module "my-untyped-module" {
 export function doSomething(): void;
 export default function main(): void;
}

// Now you can import it
import main from "my-untyped-module"; // OK

Examples

// Example 1: Missing @types package
// ERROR: Cannot find module 'lodash' or its type declarations
import _ from "lodash";
// FIX: npm install --save-dev @types/lodash

// Example 2: Custom path alias
// tsconfig.json: { "paths": { "@app/*": ["./src/*"] } }
import { helper } from "@app/utils/helper"; // needs paths config

// Example 3: Local module not found
// File: src/utils/math.ts
export function add(a: number, b: number) { return a + b; }

// File: src/index.ts
import { add } from "./utils/math"; // must match exact path and file extension

Common tsconfig.json Fixes

{
 "compilerOptions": {
 "moduleResolution": "bundler",
 "allowImportingTsExtensions": true,
 "noEmit": true,
 "baseUrl": ".",
 "paths": {
 "@/*": ["./src/*"]
 }
 },
 "include": ["src/**/*"]
}

Related Errors

  • TS2307: Cannot find module — base variant of this error
  • TS2309: Cannot find global type — missing global type declarations
  • TS2688: Cannot find type definition file — broken @types path

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript Cannot Instantiate Abstract Class — Abstract Class Usage Fix
item.description:
TypeScript: Cannot instantiate abstract class

This error occurs when you try to use new to create an instance of an abstract class. Abstract classes are designed to be base classes that cannot be instantiated directly — they must be subclassed by a concrete class that implements all abstract methods.

Common Causes

  • Directly instantiating an abstract class — new AbstractClass() is not allowed
  • Missing abstract method implementations — a subclass hasn’t implemented all abstract methods
  • Confusing abstract class with interface — trying to instantiate an abstract class like a concrete one
  • Factory function returning abstract type — factory returns the abstract base type

How to Fix

// Cause 1: Direct instantiation of abstract class
abstract class Animal {
 abstract speak(): string;

 describe() {
 return `Animal: ${this.speak()}`;
 }
}

const a = new Animal(); // TS2511: Cannot create an instance of an abstract class

// Fix: create a concrete subclass
class Dog extends Animal {
 speak() {
 return "Woof!";
 }
}

const dog = new Dog();
console.log(dog.describe()); // "Animal: Woof!"

// Cause 2: Subclass missing abstract method
class Cat extends Animal {
 // TS2515: Non-abstract class 'Cat' does not implement all abstract members
}

// Fix: implement all abstract methods
class Cat extends Animal {
 speak() {
 return "Meow!";
 }
}

// Example: Abstract class with multiple abstract methods
abstract class Shape {
 abstract area(): number;
 abstract perimeter(): number;

 describe() {
 return `Area: ${this.area()}, Perimeter: ${this.perimeter()}`;
 }
}

class Circle extends Shape {
 constructor(private radius: number) {
 super();
 }

 area() {
 return Math.PI * this.radius ** 2;
 }

 perimeter() {
 return 2 * Math.PI * this.radius;
 }
}

class Rectangle extends Shape {
 constructor(private width: number, private height: number) {
 super();
 }

 area() {
 return this.width * this.height;
 }

 perimeter() {
 return 2 * (this.width + this.height);
 }
}

function printShapeInfo(shape: Shape) {
 console.log(shape.describe());
}

printShapeInfo(new Circle(5));
printShapeInfo(new Rectangle(4, 6));

Examples

// Example 1: Abstract class as type (allowed)
abstract class Vehicle {
 abstract start(): void;
 abstract stop(): void;
}

// This is fine — using abstract class as a type annotation
function processVehicle(v: Vehicle) {
 v.start();
 v.stop();
}

// Example 2: Abstract class in factory pattern
abstract class Logger {
 abstract log(message: string): void;

 static create(type: "console" | "file"): Logger {
 if (type === "console") return new ConsoleLogger();
 return new FileLogger();
 }
}

class ConsoleLogger extends Logger {
 log(message: string) {
 console.log(message);
 }
}

class FileLogger extends Logger {
 log(message: string) {
 // write to file
 }
}

// Example 3: Abstract class with constructor
abstract class Repository<T> {
 protected items: T[] = [];

 abstract findById(id: number): T | undefined;

 add(item: T) {
 this.items.push(item);
 }

 getAll() {
 return [...this.items];
 }
}

class UserRepository extends Repository<{ id: number; name: string }> {
 findById(id: number) {
 return this.items.find(item => item.id === id);
 }
}

Related Errors

  • TS2515: Non-abstract class does not implement abstract members — missing implementation
  • TS2345: Argument type not assignable — abstract type passed where concrete expected
  • TS2322: Type not assignable — abstract class assigned to concrete type

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript Conditional Type Resolution Failed — Conditional Type Error Fix
item.description:
TypeScript: Conditional type resolution failed

A conditional type error occurs when a conditional type (T extends U ? X : Y) cannot be resolved because the type parameter is not narrowed, the infer keyword is used incorrectly, or the conditional type creates an infinitely recursive type. These errors appear when working with advanced generic types and type-level programming.

Common Causes

  • Non-narrowable type parameter — T is unknown or a union, and the conditional doesn’t distribute
  • Incorrect infer usage — infer in a position TypeScript can’t resolve
  • Recursive conditional types — a conditional type refers to itself without a base case
  • Type instantiation too deep — deeply nested conditional types exceed TypeScript’s recursion limit

How to Fix

// Cause 1: Non-distributed conditional type
type IsString<T> = T extends string ? true : false;

type A = IsString<string | number>; // should be true | false = boolean
// Sometimes this resolves to 'boolean' instead of the expected union

// Fix: use distributive conditional type with naked type parameter
type IsString<T> = T extends string ? true : false;

// Cause 2: Incorrect infer position
type ReturnType<T> = T extends (...args: any[]) => infer R ? R : never;

type X = ReturnType<string>; // string doesn't extend function → never

// Fix: handle non-function types
type SafeReturnType<T> = T extends (...args: any[]) => infer R ? R : never;
type Y = SafeReturnType<() => string>; // string

// Cause 3: Recursive conditional type without base case
type DeepReadonly<T> = T extends object
 ? { [K in keyof T]: DeepReadonly<T[K]> } // infinite if T is circular
 : T;

// Fix: add a base case or limit recursion depth
type DeepReadonly<T> = T extends object
 ? { readonly [K in keyof T]: DeepReadonly<T[K]> }
 : T;

Examples

// Example 1: Extracting function return type
type Unwrap<T> = T extends Promise<infer U>
 ? U extends Promise<infer V>
 ? V
 : U
 : T;

type A = Unwrap<Promise<Promise<string>>>; // string
type B = Unwrap<number>; // number

// Example 2: Filtering array element types
type FilterStrings<T extends any[]> = T extends [infer Head, ...infer Tail]
 ? Head extends string
 ? [Head, ...FilterStrings<Tail>]
 : FilterStrings<Tail>
 : [];

type C = FilterStrings<[1, "a", 2, "b"]>; // ["a", "b"]

// Example 3: Pattern matching with conditional types
type ExtractProperty<T, K extends string> = T extends { [P in K]: infer V }
 ? V
 : never;

type D = ExtractProperty<{ name: string; age: number }, "name">; // string
type E = ExtractProperty<{ name: string }, "missing">; // never

Related Errors

  • TS2589: Type instantiation is excessively deep — recursion limit hit
  • TS2344: Type does not satisfy constraint — generic constraint violation
  • TS2315: Generic type requires type arguments — missing generic parameters

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript Decorator Metadata Error — Experimental Decorators Fix
item.description:
TypeScript: Decorator metadata error

Decorator metadata errors occur when TypeScript’s decorator support is not properly configured, when decorators are used without enabling experimentalDecorators, or when emitDecoratorMetadata is needed for runtime type reflection. These errors are common in frameworks like NestJS, TypeORM, and Angular that rely heavily on decorators.

Common Causes

  • experimentalDecorators not enabled — decorators require this compiler option
  • emitDecoratorMetadata not enabled — needed for runtime type information in decorators
  • Decorator type mismatch — using a class decorator on a method or vice versa
  • Decorators used in a non-decorator context — using @ syntax without decorator support

How to Fix

// Fix 1: Enable decorators in tsconfig.json
{
 "compilerOptions": {
 "experimentalDecorators": true,
 "emitDecoratorMetadata": true
 }
}

// Fix 2: Correct decorator types
function Logger(target: any, propertyKey?: string, descriptor?: PropertyDescriptor) {
 if (propertyKey === undefined) {
 // Class decorator
 console.log(`Class ${target.name} created`);
 } else if (descriptor) {
 // Method decorator
 const original = descriptor.value;
 descriptor.value = function (...args: any[]) {
 console.log(`Calling ${propertyKey} with`, args);
 return original.apply(this, args);
 };
 } else {
 // Property decorator
 console.log(`Property ${propertyKey} decorated`);
 }
}

@Logger
class UserService {
 @Logger
 name: string = "Alice";

 @Logger
 getUser(id: number) {
 return { id, name: this.name };
 }
}

// Fix 3: Use correct decorator signature for each context
// Class decorator
function sealed(constructor: Function) {
 Object.seal(constructor);
 Object.seal(constructor.prototype);
}

// Method decorator
function log(target: any, key: string, descriptor: PropertyDescriptor) {
 const original = descriptor.value;
 descriptor.value = function (...args: any[]) {
 console.log(`Method ${key} called`);
 return original.apply(this, args);
 };
}

// Property decorator
function validate(target: any, key: string) {
 let value: any;
 const getter = () => value;
 const setter = (newVal: any) => {
 if (typeof newVal !== "string") {
 throw new TypeError(`Property ${key} must be a string`);
 }
 value = newVal;
 };
 Object.defineProperty(target, key, {
 get: getter,
 set: setter,
 });
}

Examples

// Example 1: NestJS-style decorator (requires emitDecoratorMetadata)
import { Injectable } from "@nestjs/common";

@Injectable()
class CatsService {
 findAll(): string[] {
 return ["Tabby", "Persian"];
 }
}

// Example 2: TypeORM entity decorator
import { Entity, PrimaryGeneratedColumn, Column } from "typeorm";

@Entity()
class User {
 @PrimaryGeneratedColumn()
 id: number;

 @Column()
 name: string;
}

// Example 3: Custom parameter decorator
function Body(key: string) {
 return (target: any, propertyKey: string | symbol, parameterIndex: number) => {
 const existingMetadata = Reflect.getMetadata("body", target, propertyKey) || {};
 existingMetadata[parameterIndex] = key;
 Reflect.defineMetadata("body", existingMetadata, target, propertyKey);
 };
}

Related Errors

  • TS1219: Experimental decorators are not enabled — missing experimentalDecorators
  • TS2688: Cannot find type definition file — missing reflect-metadata types
  • TS2345: Argument type not assignable — decorator function type mismatch

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript Generic Type Assignment Error — Generic Constraint Violation Fix
item.description:
TypeScript: Type ‘X’ is not assignable to generic type parameter

This error occurs when you pass a type to a generic function or class that doesn’t satisfy the generic’s constraints. Unlike simple type assignment errors, generic errors involve type parameters that may have extends clauses, default values, or complex variance rules.

Common Causes

  • Generic constraint violated — passing a type that doesn’t extend the constraint type
  • Covariance/contravariance issues — passing a derived type where a base type is expected in a callback position
  • Default type mismatch — the inferred type doesn’t match the generic’s default
  • Complex mapped/conditional type — the resulting type from a mapped type doesn’t match the target

How to Fix

// Cause 1: Generic constraint violation
function first<T extends { length: number }>(arr: T): T {
 return arr;
}

first(42); // TS2345: number not assignable to { length: number }

// Fix: pass a type that satisfies the constraint
first([1, 2, 3]); // OK — arrays have .length

// Cause 2: Generic with interface constraint
interface HasId {
 id: number;
}

function findById<T extends HasId>(items: T[], id: number): T | undefined {
 return items.find(item => item.id === id);
}

const users = [{ name: "Alice" }]; // missing 'id'
findById(users, 1); // TS2345: { name: string } not assignable to HasId

// Fix: ensure items have the required property
const users = [{ id: 1, name: "Alice" }];
findById(users, 1); // OK

// Cause 3: Returning wrong type from generic function
function identity<T>(x: T): T {
 return x;
}

function broken<T>(x: T): T {
 return "hello" as T; // TS2322: string not assignable to T
}

// Fix: return the actual parameter
function correct<T>(x: T): T {
 return x;
}

Examples

// Example 1: Generic class with constraint
class Container<T extends object> {
 constructor(public value: T) {}
}

new Container(42); // TS2344: number doesn't satisfy 'object'

// Fix
new Container({ data: 42 });

// Example 2: Generic function with multiple constraints
function merge<T extends object, U extends object>(a: T, b: U): T & U {
 return { ...a, ...b };
}

merge({ x: 1 }, { y: "hello" }); // OK
merge({ x: 1 }, [1, 2, 3]); // TS2345: array not assignable to object

// Example 3: Conditional type result doesn't match
type Unwrap<T> = T extends Promise<infer U> ? U : T;

function process<T>(val: Unwrap<T>): void {}

process<string>("hello"); // OK — Unwrap<string> is string
process<Promise<number>>(42); // TS2345: number not assignable to Unwrap<Promise<number>>

Related Errors

  • TS2322: Type not assignable — non-generic type assignment
  • TS2345: Argument type not assignable — argument in generic context
  • TS2749: Referenced value is not a class — using non-class in generic context

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript Implicit Any Type — Expression Has No Index Signature Fix
item.description:
TypeScript: Element implicitly has an ‘any’ type because expression of type ‘X’ is not of type ‘Y’

This error occurs when you access a property on an object using a dynamic key, but the object’s type doesn’t have an index signature that allows that key. TypeScript cannot guarantee the key exists, so it would implicitly be any.

Common Causes

  • Dynamic property access without index signature — obj[dynamicKey] where obj has no index signature
  • Using a variable as a key on a typed object — record[key] where key isn’t a known literal
  • Enum used as object key — obj[enumValue] where the object type doesn’t include the enum
  • Dot notation with dynamic string — accessing properties that aren’t declared in the interface

How to Fix

// Cause 1: Dynamic access on typed object
interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}

const user: User = { name: "Alice", age: 30 };
const field = "name";
console.log(user[field]); // TS7053: Element implicitly has an 'any' type

// Fix 1a: Add index signature
interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
 [key: string]: string | number;
}

// Fix 1b: Use type assertion
console.log(user[field as keyof User]);

// Cause 2: Record access without proper typing
const scores: Record<string, number> = { math: 90, science: 85 };
const key = "math";
console.log(scores[key]); // this works because Record has an index signature

// Cause 3: Enum as key
enum Color { Red = "red", Blue = "blue" }
const palette: Record<Color, string> = {
 [Color.Red]: "#ff0000",
 [Color.Blue]: "#0000ff",
};

const c = Color.Red;
console.log(palette[c]); // this works with Record<Color, string>

Examples

// Example 1: Object literal without index signature
interface Config {
 host: string;
 port: number;
}

const config: Config = { host: "localhost", port: 3000 };
const keys: string[] = ["host", "port"];

keys.forEach(key => {
 console.log(config[key]); // TS7053
});

// Fix: use keyof assertion
keys.forEach(key => {
 console.log(config[key as keyof Config]);
});

// Example 2: Nested dynamic access
interface Nested {
 a: { b: { c: number } };
}

const obj: Nested = { a: { b: { c: 42 } } };
const path = ["a", "b", "c"];

// TypeScript can't verify this path is valid
path.reduce((acc, key) => acc[key], obj); // TS7053

// Fix: use type assertion
path.reduce((acc: any, key) => acc[key], obj) as any;

Related Errors

  • TS7006: Parameter has no type annotation — implicit any on parameters
  • TS7031: Binding element has implicit any — destructured parameters without types
  • TS2339: Property does not exist on type — static access to non-existent property

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript Intersection Type Error — Conflicting Property Types Fix
item.description:
TypeScript: Intersection type error

An intersection type error occurs when combining two types with & produces a type with conflicting property types — for example, string & number becomes never for that property. This often happens when merging interfaces, combining third-party types, or accidentally creating impossible types.

Common Causes

  • Conflicting property types — A & B where A.x: string and B.x: number produces x: never
  • Merging incompatible interfaces — two interfaces with the same property but different types
  • Library type conflicts — combining types from two libraries that define the same property differently
  • Incorrect mapped type — a mapped type produces an intersection with conflicting types

How to Fix

// Cause 1: Conflicting property types
interface A {
 value: string;
}

interface B {
 value: number;
}

type C = A & B; // value is never — string & number = never

// Fix: use a union or choose one type
type C = A | B; // value is string | number

// Cause 2: Method signature conflicts
interface Logger {
 log(msg: string): void;
}

interface VerboseLogger {
 log(msg: string, level: number): void;
}

type Combined = Logger & VerboseLogger;
// log is (msg: string) => void & (msg: string, level: number) => void = never

// Fix: make the method signatures compatible
interface Logger {
 log(msg: string, level?: number): void;
}

// Cause 3: Third-party type conflict
interface React.CSSProperties {
 display: string;
}

interface TailwindProps {
 display: "flex" | "block" | "none";
}

// Fix: use declaration merging or override

Examples

// Example 1: Intersection of primitives is never
type Impossible = string & number; // never
type AlsoImpossible = boolean & string; // never

// Example 2: Function type intersection
type Fn1 = (x: number) => string;
type Fn2 = (x: string) => number;
type Combined = Fn1 & Fn2;
// Combined requires x to be both number AND string → x: never

// Example 3: Correct intersection usage
interface Person {
 name: string;
}

interface Employee {
 employeeId: number;
}

type PersonEmployee = Person & Employee; // { name: string; employeeId: number }
// This works because there are no conflicting properties

const pe: PersonEmployee = { name: "Alice", employeeId: 123 };

// Example 4: Resolving conflicts with Pick/Omit
interface ConfigA {
 port: number;
 host: string;
}

interface ConfigB {
 port: string; // conflicts with ConfigA.port
}

// Fix: override the conflicting property
type MergedConfig = Omit<ConfigA, "port"> & ConfigB;
// port is string (from ConfigB), host is string (from ConfigA)

Related Errors

  • TS2322: Type not assignable — resulting type can’t be assigned
  • TS2345: Argument type not assignable — intersection passed as argument
  • TS2589: Type instantiation is excessively deep — recursive intersection types

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript Member Not Found in Enum — Enum Value Access Fix
item.description:
TypeScript: Member not found in enum

This error occurs when you try to access a member on an enum value that doesn’t exist, or when you try to use a string enum value as a number enum, or access an enum via bracket notation with an invalid key.

Common Causes

  • Typo in enum member name — accessing Color.Reed instead of Color.Red
  • Using numeric enum value as key — trying to access Color[2] when only values 0 and 1 exist
  • String enum accessed as numeric — string enums don’t support reverse mapping
  • Accessing enum member that was removed — refactoring removed a member but references remain

How to Fix

// Cause 1: Typo in member name
enum Color {
 Red = "red",
 Green = "green",
 Blue = "blue",
}

const c = Color.Reed; // TS2339: Property 'Reed' does not exist on typeof Color

// Fix: check the correct name
const c = Color.Red;

// Cause 2: Numeric enum — invalid key access
enum Direction {
 Up, // 0
 Down, // 1
 Left, // 2
}

const name = Direction[3]; // TS7053: Element implicitly has an 'any' type — no index 3

// Fix: only access valid numeric keys
const name = Direction[0]; // "Up"

// Cause 3: String enum reverse mapping doesn't work
enum Status {
 Active = "active",
 Inactive = "inactive",
}

const key = Status.Active; // "active"
const reverse = Status["active"]; // TS2339: doesn't work with string enums

// Fix: use Record or string lookup
const reverseMap: Record<string, keyof typeof Status> = {
 active: "Active",
 inactive: "Inactive",
};

Examples

// Example 1: Iterating enum members
enum Fruit {
 Apple = "apple",
 Banana = "banana",
 Cherry = "cherry",
}

// Get all values
const values = Object.values(Fruit); // ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]

// Get all keys
const keys = Object.keys(Fruit); // ["Apple", "Banana", "Cherry"]

// Example 2: Type-safe enum check
function processColor(color: Color): void {
 switch (color) {
 case Color.Red:
 console.log("Red");
 break;
 case Color.Green:
 console.log("Green");
 break;
 case Color.Blue:
 console.log("Blue");
 break;
 default:
 const _exhaustive: never = color; // compile error if case is missing
 }
}

// Example 3: Enum as object key
const config: Record<Status, string> = {
 [Status.Active]: "Config for active",
 [Status.Inactive]: "Config for inactive",
};

Related Errors

  • TS2339: Property does not exist on type — general property access on type
  • TS2322: Type not assignable — enum value assigned to wrong type
  • TS2749: Referenced value is not a class — enum used as a class

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript Missing Property in Type — Structural Type Error Fix
item.description:
TypeScript: Property ‘X’ is missing in type

This error occurs when an object literal is missing a property that the target type requires. TypeScript’s structural type system requires that every required property in the target interface is present in the source type.

Common Causes

  • Missing required interface properties — not all fields of an interface are provided
  • Function return type mismatch — a function returns an object missing required fields
  • Spreading incomplete objects — spreading an object that doesn’t have all required properties
  • API response typing — typing a response more strictly than the actual data

How to Fix

interface User {
 id: number;
 name: string;
 email: string;
 role: "admin" | "user";
}

// Cause 1: Missing properties in object literal
const user: User = {
 id: 1,
 name: "Alice",
 // TS2741: Property 'email' is missing
};

// Fix: provide all required properties
const user: User = {
 id: 1,
 name: "Alice",
 email: "alice@example.com",
 role: "user",
};

// Cause 2: Partial object from a function
function getUser(): User {
 return { id: 1, name: "Alice" }; // TS2741: missing email and role
}

// Fix: ensure function returns all required fields
function getUser(): User {
 return { id: 1, name: "Alice", email: "alice@example.com", role: "user" };
}

// Cause 3: Make properties optional if they aren't always needed
interface User {
 id: number;
 name: string;
 email?: string; // optional
 role?: "admin" | "user"; // optional
}

Examples

interface Product {
 id: number;
 name: string;
 price: number;
 description: string;
}

// Spread incomplete object
const partial = { id: 1, name: "Widget" };
const product: Product = { ...partial, price: 9.99 }; // TS2741: missing description

// Fix: include all fields
const product: Product = { ...partial, price: 9.99, description: "A widget" };

// Array of objects — one is missing a field
const items: Product[] = [
 { id: 1, name: "A", price: 10, description: "desc" },
 { id: 2, name: "B", price: 20 }, // TS2741: missing description
];

Related Errors

  • TS2322: Type not assignable — broader assignability issues
  • TS2339: Property does not exist on type — accessing extra/wrong properties
  • TS2740: Type missing properties — related variant for class types

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript Object Possibly Undefined — Null Check Required Fix
item.description:
TypeScript: Object is possibly ‘undefined’

This error occurs when you access a property or method on a value that TypeScript knows could be undefined. It is triggered by strictNullChecks and tells you the variable might not have been assigned a value yet.

Common Causes

  • Uninitialized optional property — accessing a property on an optional type without checking
  • Array access without bounds check — arr[index] returns T | undefined
  • Map or object lookup — obj[key] may return undefined if the key doesn’t exist
  • Async data not yet loaded — variable is undefined until a promise resolves

How to Fix

interface User {
 name: string;
 address?: {
 street?: string;
 city: string;
 };
}

// Cause 1: Accessing optional property without check
function getCity(user: User): string {
 return user.address.city; // TS2532: Object is possibly 'undefined'
}

// Fix: optional chaining
function getCity(user: User): string {
 return user.address?.city ?? "Unknown";
}

// Cause 2: Array access
const items = [1, 2, 3];
const first = items[10]; // type is number | undefined

// Fix: check before use
if (items[10] !== undefined) {
 console.log(items[10]);
}

// Cause 3: Promise result not awaited
let user: User | undefined;
fetchUser().then(u => user = u);
console.log(user.name); // user is undefined here

// Fix: await or check
const user = await fetchUser();
console.log(user.name);

Examples

// Deeply nested optional access
interface Company {
 ceo?: {
 name?: string;
 };
}

function getCEOName(company: Company): string {
 return company.ceo.name; // TS2532
}

// Fix with optional chaining
function getCEOName(company: Company): string {
 return company.ceo?.name ?? "Unknown CEO";
}

// Map lookup
const map = new Map<string, number>();
const value = map.get("key"); // number | undefined
console.log(value + 1); // TS2532

// Fix
const value = map.get("key") ?? 0;
console.log(value + 1);

Related Errors

  • TS2339: Property does not exist on type — accessing a property that doesn’t exist at all
  • TS2531: Object is possibly null — similar but for null
  • TS18046: Variable is of type unknown — accessing properties on unknown

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS1005 — Semicolon Expected
item.description:
TS1005 — ‘;’ expected

TS1005 is a parse error indicating TypeScript encountered a token where it expected a semicolon. This usually means there’s a syntax issue with how statements, declarations, or expressions are structured.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Missing semicolon after variable declaration
let x = 10
let y = 20 // TS1005 on this line — expected ';' after declaration

// Cause 2: Missing semicolon in for loop
for (let i = 0; i < 10; i++)
{ // TS1005 — expected ';' after for statement

// Cause 3: Missing semicolon after interface property
interface User {
 name: string
 age: number // TS1005 if strict semicolon enforcement
}

// Cause 4: Missing semicolon in import statement
import { helper } from "./utils"
import { other } from "./other" // TS1005

Solutions

Fix 1: Add missing semicolons

// Wrong
let x = 10
let y = 20

// Correct
let x = 10;
let y = 20;

Fix 2: Use a linter to enforce semicolons

// .eslintrc.json
{
 "rules": {
 "semi": ["error", "always"]
 }
}

Fix 3: Check for missing closing brackets or braces

// Wrong — missing closing parenthesis
const result = add(1, 2
// TS1005: expected ';' — really means missing ')'

// Correct
const result = add(1, 2);

Fix 4: Fix malformed expressions

// Wrong — double operator
let x = 10 ++ 5; // TS1005

// Correct
let x = 10 + 5;

Examples

// Example 1: Accidentally using a keyword
let class = "myClass"; // TS1005: ';' expected (class is reserved)

// Example 2: Forgetting semicolon in chained method
const result = items
 .filter(x => x.active)
 .map(x => x.name) // OK with ASI
 .join(", "); // TS1005 if context requires semicolon

// Fix: add semicolon at end
const result = items
 .filter(x => x.active)
 .map(x => x.name)
 .join(", ");

Related Errors

  • TS1109: Expression expected — related parse error.
  • TS1128: Declaration or statement expected — broader parse error.
  • TS1434: Unexpected keyword or identifier — unexpected token.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS1109 — Expression Expected
item.description:
TS1109 — Expression expected

TS1109 is a parse error indicating TypeScript encountered a token where it expected an expression. This usually happens with malformed function calls, missing operands, or incomplete code.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Empty function call argument
const result = Math.random() // Missing closing paren or argument

// Cause 2: Missing right operand
let x = 5 + ; // TS1109: expression expected after '+'

// Cause 3: Incomplete arrow function
const fn = () => // TS1109: expression expected after '=>'

// Cause 4: Missing value in assignment
const obj = {
 key: // TS1109: expression expected after ':'
};

Solutions

Fix 1: Complete the expression

// Wrong
let x = 5 + ;

// Correct
let x = 5 + 10;

Fix 2: Close parentheses and provide arguments

// Wrong
const result = Math.random()

// Correct
const result = Math.random();

Fix 3: Provide a return value for arrow functions

// Wrong
const fn = () =>

// Correct
const fn = () => 42;

// Or with block body
const fn = () => {
 return 42;
};

Fix 4: Complete object property values

// Wrong
const obj = {
 key:
};

// Correct
const obj = {
 key: "value"
};

Examples

// Example 1: Incomplete template literal
const name = `Hello, ${; // TS1109

// Fix:
const name = `Hello, ${user.name}`;

// Example 2: Missing argument in new expression
const el = new HTMLElement(); // If HTMLElement isn't defined
// TS1109 if the expression can't be resolved

Related Errors

  • TS1005: Semicolon expected — missing semicolons.
  • TS1128: Declaration or statement expected — broader token error.
  • TS1434: Unexpected keyword or identifier — unexpected tokens.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS1128 — Declaration or Statement Expected
item.description:
TS1128 — Declaration or statement expected

TS1128 occurs when TypeScript encounters a token where it expects a declaration (variable, function, class, etc.) or a statement. This often indicates a structural syntax problem like extra closing braces, misplaced code, or invalid top-level constructs.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Extra closing brace
function greet() {
 console.log("hello");
}} // TS1128: extra '}' — declaration or statement expected

// Cause 2: Code outside of a function or class at invalid location
function process() {}
 const x = 10; // TS1128: unexpected indentation / misplaced code

// Cause 3: Missing function body or expression
function compute() // TS1128: expected '{' or '=>'

// Cause 4: Invalid top-level expression
42 // TS1128 if not in a module or script context properly

Solutions

Fix 1: Remove extra closing braces

// Wrong
function greet() {
 console.log("hello");
}}

// Correct
function greet() {
 console.log("hello");
}

Fix 2: Ensure code is inside proper blocks

// Wrong
function process() {}
 const x = 10; // outside the function

// Correct
function process() {
 const x = 10; // inside the function
}

Fix 3: Complete function declarations

// Wrong
function compute()

// Correct
function compute(): number {
 return 42;
}

Fix 4: Check for misplaced imports or exports

// Wrong — export outside module context
export default {} // TS1128 if file is not a module

// Correct — ensure the file has at least one import/export
import { something } from "./other";
export default {};

Examples

// Example 1: Accidentally closing a block early
if (condition) {
 doSomething();
} // Extra close
doOtherThing(); // TS1128

// Example 2: Mixing declaration and expression
const obj = {
 method() {}
}
const x = 10 // Missing semicolon, may cause TS1128 on next line

Related Errors

  • TS1005: Semicolon expected — missing semicolons.
  • TS1109: Expression expected — incomplete expressions.
  • TS1434: Unexpected keyword or identifier — unexpected tokens.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS1434 — Unexpected Keyword or Identifier
item.description:
TS1434 — Unexpected keyword or identifier

TS1434 occurs when TypeScript encounters a keyword or identifier in a position where it doesn’t belong according to the grammar rules. This is typically caused by using reserved words in invalid contexts or placing identifiers where expressions or declarations are expected.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Using a reserved keyword as an identifier
let class = "myClass"; // TS1434: Unexpected keyword 'class'

// Cause 2: Misplaced 'return' outside a function
return 42; // TS1434 if at top level

// Cause 3: Using 'type' as a variable name (pre-5.0)
type = 10; // TS1434: 'type' is a reserved keyword in TypeScript

// Cause 4: Keyword in wrong position in type annotation
type Foo = public string; // TS1434: 'public' not valid here

Solutions

Fix 1: Rename the identifier to avoid reserved words

// Wrong
let class = "myClass";

// Correct
let className = "myClass";

Fix 2: Move return statements inside functions

// Wrong
return 42;

// Correct
function getValue() {
 return 42;
}

Fix 3: Use different variable names for reserved words

// Wrong — 'type' is reserved in TypeScript
let type = "foo";

// Correct
let typeValue = "foo";

Fix 4: Remove invalid modifiers from type annotations

// Wrong
type Foo = public string;

// Correct — access modifiers are for class members, not type aliases
type Foo = string;

Examples

// Example 1: Using 'const' in wrong context
interface Config {
 const host: string; // TS1434: 'const' not valid in interface
}

// Fix: remove 'const'
interface Config {
 host: string;
}

// Example 2: Using 'async' outside function
async const result = fetch("/api"); // TS1434

// Fix: use async function or async IIFE
const result = (async () => fetch("/api"))();

Related Errors

  • TS1435: Unknown keyword or identifier — unrecognized keyword.
  • TS1005: Semicolon expected — related parse error.
  • TS1128: Declaration or statement expected — broader parse error.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS1435 — Unknown Keyword or Identifier
item.description:
TS1435 — Unknown keyword or identifier

TS1435 occurs when TypeScript encounters an identifier that it doesn’t recognize as a valid keyword or identifier in the current context. This often means a typo in a keyword, using a future/reserved word, or an unrecognized syntax.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Typo in a keyword
const x = 10;
pritn(x); // TS1435: 'pritn' is not recognized (typo of 'print')

// Cause 2: Using a keyword from another language
def greet(): // TS1435: 'def' is not a TypeScript keyword (Python syntax)

// Cause 3: Unrecognized decorator or modifier
@sealed // TS1435 if decorator not recognized or not enabled
class MyClass {}

// Cause 4: Misspelled type annotation keyword
let x: numberr = 10; // TS1435: 'numberr' is not a valid type

Solutions

Fix 1: Correct keyword typos

// Wrong
pritn(x);

// Correct
console.log(x);

Fix 2: Use valid TypeScript keywords

// Wrong — Python syntax
def greet():

// Correct — TypeScript function
function greet(): void {}

Fix 3: Enable decorator support if needed

// tsconfig.json
{
 "compilerOptions": {
 "experimentalDecorators": true,
 "emitDecoratorMetadata": true
 }
}

Fix 4: Fix type annotation typos

// Wrong
let x: numberr = 10;

// Correct
let x: number = 10;

Examples

// Example 1: Using a non-existent type
let data: Arrray<number> = []; // TS1435: 'Arrray' not recognized

// Fix:
let data: Array<number> = [];

// Example 2: Wrong loop syntax from another language
for i in range(10) { // TS1435: not valid TypeScript
}

// Fix:
for (let i = 0; i < 10; i++) {}

Related Errors

  • TS1434: Unexpected keyword or identifier — keyword in wrong position.
  • TS2304: Cannot find name — identifier not in scope.
  • TS1128: Declaration or statement expected — broader parse error.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS18046 — 'X' Is of Type 'unknown'
item.description:
TS18046 — ‘X’ is of type ‘unknown’

TS18046 occurs when you try to use a value typed as unknown without first narrowing its type. The unknown type is TypeScript’s type-safe alternative to any — you must verify the type before accessing properties or methods.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Catch clause variable is unknown by default
try {
 throw new Error("fail");
} catch (e) {
 console.log(e.message); // TS18046: 'e' is of type 'unknown'
}

// Cause 2: Using unknown from a generic
function parse<T>(input: unknown): T {
 return input; // TS18046 if you try to access input properties
}

// Cause 3: Fetching untyped data
const response = await fetch("/api");
const data = await response.json(); // data is 'any', but if typed as 'unknown'...
console.log(data.name); // TS18046

// Cause 4: Value from a Map with unknown value type
const map = new Map<string, unknown>();
const val = map.get("key");
console.log(val.toString()); // TS18046

Solutions

Fix 1: Use a type guard to narrow the type

// Wrong
try {
 throw new Error("fail");
} catch (e) {
 console.log(e.message); // TS18046
}

// Correct
try {
 throw new Error("fail");
} catch (e) {
 if (e instanceof Error) {
 console.log(e.message); // OK — e is Error
 }
}

Fix 2: Use typeof checks for primitives

function process(value: unknown) {
 if (typeof value === "string") {
 console.log(value.toUpperCase()); // OK — value is string
 }
}

Fix 3: Use type assertions when you know the type

const data = await response.json() as User;
console.log(data.name); // OK

Fix 4: Use a validated parsing library

import { z } from "zod";

const UserSchema = z.object({
 name: z.string(),
 age: z.number(),
});

const data = UserSchema.parse(await response.json());
console.log(data.name); // OK — typed as { name: string; age: number }

Examples

// Example 1: forEach with unknown
const items: unknown[] = [1, "hello", true];
items.forEach(item => {
 console.log(item.toFixed()); // TS18046
});

// Fix:
items.forEach(item => {
 if (typeof item === "number") {
 console.log(item.toFixed()); // OK
 }
});

// Example 2: JSON.parse returns any, but explicit unknown needs narrowing
const parsed: unknown = JSON.parse('{"x": 10}');
console.log(parsed.x); // TS18046

Related Errors

  • TS7006: Parameter implicitly has ‘any’ type — the opposite: no type at all vs strict unknown.
  • TS2322: Type is not assignable — broader assignability issues.
  • TS2531: Object is possibly ’null’ — similar narrowing needed for null.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2300 — Duplicate Identifier
item.description:
TS2300 — Duplicate identifier

TS2300 occurs when the same identifier is declared more than once in the same scope. TypeScript doesn’t allow redeclaring a variable, parameter, or type name that already exists in the current block or file.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Redeclaring a variable in the same scope
let x = 10;
let x = 20; // TS2300: Duplicate identifier 'x'

// Cause 2: Parameter name conflicts with variable
let name = "Alice";
function greet(name: string) {} // TS2304 if 'name' is in outer scope with let/const

// Cause 3: Duplicate interface declarations
interface Config {
 host: string;
}
interface Config { // TS2300: Duplicate identifier
 port: number;
}

// Cause 4: Importing the same name from different modules
import { helper } from "./a";
import { helper } from "./b"; // TS2304 if both export 'helper'

Solutions

Fix 1: Rename the duplicate identifier

// Wrong
let x = 10;
let x = 20;

// Correct
let x = 10;
let x2 = 20;

Fix 2: Use block scoping to avoid conflicts

let name = "Alice";

function greet() {
 let name = "Bob"; // OK — different scope
 console.log(name);
}

Fix 3: Merge interface declarations

// Wrong
interface Config {
 host: string;
}
interface Config {
 port: number;
}

// Correct — merge into one
interface Config {
 host: string;
 port: number;
}

Fix 4: Rename imports to avoid conflicts

import { helper as helperA } from "./a";
import { helper as helperB } from "./b";

Examples

// Example 1: Redeclaring in for loop
for (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) {}
for (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) {} // TS2300: 'i' already declared

// Fix: use different variable names
for (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) {}
for (let j = 0; j < 5; j++) {}

// Example 2: Conflicting class and namespace
class MyClass {}
namespace MyClass {} // TS2300 in some configurations

Related Errors

  • TS2301: Duplicate function implementation — duplicate function bodies.
  • TS2309: An export assignment cannot be used in a module — export syntax conflicts.
  • TS2464: A class can only implement an identifier — related identifier issues.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2301 — Duplicate Function Implementation
item.description:
TS2301 — Duplicate function implementation

TS2301 occurs when the same function is implemented more than once in the same scope. This differs from function overloads, which require different parameter signatures. Duplicate implementations mean the exact same signature appears twice.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Defining the same function twice
function greet(name: string) {
 return `Hello, ${name}`;
}
function greet(name: string) { // TS2301: Duplicate function implementation
 return `Hi, ${name}`;
}

// Cause 2: Arrow function reassigned in same scope
const add = (a: number, b: number) => a + b;
const add = (a: number, b: number) => a + b; // TS2301

// Cause 3: Method defined twice in a class
class Service {
 process() { return 1; }
 process() { return 2; } // TS2301
}

// Cause 4: Duplicate function in module augmentation
declare module "foo" {
 function bar(): void;
 function bar(): string; // TS2301 — same signature
}

Solutions

Fix 1: Remove the duplicate implementation

// Wrong — two implementations
function greet(name: string) {
 return `Hello, ${name}`;
}
function greet(name: string) {
 return `Hi, ${name}`;
}

// Correct — keep only one
function greet(name: string) {
 return `Hello, ${name}`;
}

Fix 2: Use function overloads if you need multiple signatures

// Wrong — same signature twice
function process(input: string): string;
function process(input: string): string; // TS2301

// Correct — different signatures
function process(input: string): string;
function process(input: number): string;
function process(input: string | number): string {
 return String(input);
}

Fix 3: Rename duplicate arrow functions

// Wrong
const add = (a: number, b: number) => a + b;
const add = (a: number, b: number) => a + b;

// Correct — use a different name
const add = (a: number, b: number) => a + b;
const addVerbose = (a: number, b: number) => a + b;

Fix 4: Merge method implementations

// Wrong
class Service {
 process() { return 1; }
 process() { return 2; }
}

// Correct
class Service {
 process(mode?: string) {
 return mode === "fast" ? 2 : 1;
 }
}

Examples

// Example 1: Accidental duplicate in module
// utils.ts
export function formatDate(d: Date) { return d.toISOString(); }
export function formatDate(d: Date) { return d.toLocaleDateString(); } // TS2301

// Example 2: Namespace and function conflict
function build() {}
namespace build {} // May cause TS2301 in some configurations

Related Errors

  • TS2300: Duplicate identifier — duplicate variable/type names.
  • TS2309: An export assignment cannot be used in a module — export syntax issues.
  • TS2769: No overload matches this call — overload resolution failures.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2304 — Cannot Find Name
item.description:
TS2304 — Cannot Find Name

TS2304 occurs when TypeScript encounters an identifier that has not been declared in the current scope. This typically means a variable, function, class, or type is being used without being imported or defined.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Using a variable before declaring it
console.log(x); // TS2304: Cannot find name 'x'

// Cause 2: Missing import for a type or value
const data: User = {}; // TS2304: Cannot find name 'User'

// Cause 3: Typo in variable or function name
let userName = "Alice";
console.log(useName); // TS2304: Cannot find name 'useName'

// Cause 4: Using a global without the proper lib or type
const result = fetch("/api"); // TS2304: Cannot find name 'fetch' if lib not configured

Solutions

Fix 1: Declare the variable before using it

// Wrong
console.log(x);

// Correct
let x = 10;
console.log(x);

Fix 2: Import missing types and modules

// Wrong — User type is not imported
const user: User = { name: "Alice" };

// Correct
import { User } from "./models";
const user: User = { name: "Alice" };

Fix 3: Fix typos in identifiers

// Wrong
let userName = "Alice";
console.log(useName); // TS2304

// Correct
console.log(userName);

Fix 4: Add missing lib references in tsconfig

{
 "compilerOptions": {
 "lib": ["ES2020", "DOM"]
 }
}

Examples

// Example 1: Forgetting to import
// file: app.ts
const arr = new Array(); // TS2304 if Array is not imported

// Example 2: Using a global type without @types
const el = document.getElementById("app"); // TS2304 if DOM types are missing

// Example 3: Circular dependency causing unresolved names
// a.ts
import { b } from "./b";
export const a = b + 1;

// b.ts
import { a } from "./a";
export const b = a + 1; // May produce TS2304

Related Errors

  • TS2307: Cannot find module — module import path is wrong or missing.
  • TS2552: Cannot find name, did you mean… — similar but offers a close match.
  • TS7006: Parameter implicitly has ‘any’ type — related to missing type annotations.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2307 — Cannot Find Module
item.description:
TS2307 — Cannot Find Module

TS2307 occurs when TypeScript cannot resolve a module imported via import or require(). This can happen when the module path is incorrect, the module doesn’t exist, or type declarations are missing.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Typo in module path
import { helper } from "./helpr"; // TS2307: Cannot find module './helpr'

// Cause 2: Missing type declarations for a package
import _ from "lodash"; // TS2307 if @types/lodash is not installed

// Cause 3: Module not installed via npm
import express from "express"; // TS2307 if express is not in node_modules

// Cause 4: Incorrect relative path
import { utils } from "../utils"; // TS2307 if the path doesn't resolve

Solutions

Fix 1: Correct the import path

// Wrong
import { helper } from "./helpr";

// Correct
import { helper } from "./helper";

Fix 2: Install missing type declarations

npm install --save-dev @types/lodash
npm install --save-dev @types/express

Fix 3: Install the missing package

npm install express

Fix 4: Configure module resolution in tsconfig

{
 "compilerOptions": {
 "moduleResolution": "node",
 "baseUrl": ".",
 "paths": {
 "@app/*": ["src/*"]
 }
 }
}

Fix 5: Create a type declaration file for untyped modules

// types/custom-module.d.ts
declare module "untyped-module" {
 const value: any;
 export default value;
}

Examples

// Example 1: Missing @types package
import jwt from "jsonwebtoken";
// TS2307: Cannot find module 'jsonwebtoken' or its corresponding type declarations
// Fix: npm install --save-dev @types/jsonwebtoken

// Example 2: Incorrect relative path
// src/utils.ts exists but you import from wrong path
import { formatDate } from "./util"; // TS2307 — file is "utils", not "util"

Related Errors

  • TS2304: Cannot find name — identifier not in scope, unrelated to module resolution.
  • TS2309: An export assignment cannot be used in a module — export syntax mismatch.
  • TS7016: Could not find a declaration file for module — module exists but has no types.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2309 — Export Assignment Cannot Be Used in a Module
item.description:
TS2309 — An export assignment cannot be used in a module

TS2309 occurs when you use export = (CommonJS-style default export) in a file that TypeScript treats as an ES module. This happens when your tsconfig’s module setting is es2015/esnext but you’re using CommonJS export syntax.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Using 'export =' in ES module mode
export = {
 name: "myModule",
 version: "1.0.0",
}; // TS2309 if module is ES2015+

// Cause 2: Mixing 'export =' with named exports
export = function () {};
export const other = 1; // TS2309 — can't mix with export =

// Cause 3: 'export =' in a declaration file designed for CommonJS
// types/my-lib.d.ts
declare function myLib(): void;
export = myLib; // TS2309 if consuming file uses ES modules

// Cause 4: Importing with 'import =' when file uses export default
import myLib = require("my-lib"); // TS2309 if module resolution is ES

Solutions

Fix 1: Use export default instead of export =

// Wrong — CommonJS style
export = {
 name: "myModule",
};

// Correct — ES module style
export default {
 name: "myModule",
};

Fix 2: Use named exports

// Wrong
export = {
 name: "myModule",
 version: "1.0.0",
};

// Correct
export const name = "myModule";
export const version = "1.0.0";

Fix 3: Switch to CommonJS module resolution

// tsconfig.json
{
 "compilerOptions": {
 "module": "commonjs"
 }
}

Fix 4: Use import/export syntax compatible with your module setting

// For ES modules
import myLib from "my-lib";

// For CommonJS
import myLib = require("my-lib");

Examples

// Example 1: Declaration file with export =
// my-lib.d.ts
declare class MyClass {
 constructor();
 doSomething(): void;
}
export = MyClass; // Only works with "module": "commonjs"

// Example 2: Trying to use both export = and export default
export = something;
export default something; // TS2309 — can't have both

Related Errors

  • TS2307: Cannot find module — module import path issues.
  • TS2300: Duplicate identifier — duplicate exports.
  • TS2749: Refers to a value, used as a type — value vs type confusion.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2322 — Type Not Assignable
item.description:
TS2322 — Type ‘X’ is not assignable to type ‘Y’

TS2322 occurs when you assign a value of one type to a variable, parameter, or return position that expects a different, incompatible type. TypeScript’s structural type system checks that the source type satisfies all requirements of the target type.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Assigning a string to a number
let age: number = "twenty-five"; // TS2322: Type 'string' is not assignable to type 'number'

// Cause 2: Missing required properties
interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}
const user: User = { name: "Alice" }; // TS2322: Property 'age' is missing

// Cause 3: Assigning nullable to non-nullable
let name: string = null; // TS2322 if strictNullChecks is on

// Cause 4: Returning wrong type from function
function getLength(): number {
 return "hello"; // TS2322: Type 'string' is not assignable to type 'number'
}

Solutions

Fix 1: Use the correct type

// Wrong
let age: number = "twenty-five";

// Correct
let age: number = 25;

Fix 2: Provide all required properties

// Wrong
const user: User = { name: "Alice" };

// Correct
const user: User = { name: "Alice", age: 30 };

Fix 3: Handle nullable values properly

// Wrong
let name: string = null;

// Correct — accept null
let name: string | null = null;

// Correct — use a default
let name: string = "unknown";

Fix 4: Use type assertions when you know the type

const input = document.getElementById("name") as HTMLInputElement;
input.value = "Alice";

Examples

// Example 1: Enum mismatch
enum Color { Red, Green, Blue }
let c: Color = Color.Red;

enum Direction { Up, Down }
let d: Direction = c; // TS2322: Type 'Color' is not assignable to type 'Direction'

// Example 2: Excess properties in object literal
interface Config {
 host: string;
 port: number;
}
const config: Config = { host: "localhost", port: 3000, debug: true }; // TS2322

Related Errors

  • TS2345: Argument of type ‘X’ is not assignable — similar but for function arguments.
  • TS2741: Property is missing in type — specific variant for missing properties.
  • TS2352: Conversion may be a mistake — type assertion issues.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2339 — Property Does Not Exist on Type
item.description:
TS2339 — Property ‘X’ does not exist on type ‘Y’

TS2339 occurs when you try to access a property on a type that doesn’t declare that property. TypeScript enforces property access based on the declared shape of types, so accessing a non-existent property is a compile-time error.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Accessing a property not in the interface
interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}
const user: User = { name: "Alice", age: 30 };
console.log(user.email); // TS2339: Property 'email' does not exist on type 'User'

// Cause 2: Typo in property name
console.log(user.nme); // TS2339: Did you mean 'name'?

// Cause 3: Using 'any' bypass but the real type is stricter
const response: any = fetch("/api");
console.log(response.data.id); // Compiles with any, fails at runtime

// Cause 4: Accessing array methods on non-array
const num: number = 42;
num.map(x => x); // TS2339: Property 'map' does not exist on type 'number'

Solutions

Fix 1: Add the property to the type definition

// Wrong
interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}

// Correct — add email to the interface
interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
 email: string;
}

Fix 2: Use optional properties or index signatures

interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
 email?: string; // Optional
}

// Or allow any extra properties
interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
 [key: string]: unknown;
}

Fix 3: Fix typos in property names

// Wrong
console.log(user.nme);

// Correct
console.log(user.name);

Fix 4: Use type guards before accessing properties

function processUser(user: User | Admin) {
 if ("email" in user) {
 console.log(user.email); // OK — TypeScript knows it's Admin
 }
}

Examples

// Example 1: API response typed too narrowly
interface ApiResponse {
 data: string;
}
const res: ApiResponse = { data: "hello" };
console.log(res.data.length); // TS2339 if data could be undefined

// Example 2: Class without a property
class Animal {
 name: string = "";
}
const cat: Animal = new Animal();
cat.sound = "meow"; // TS2339: Property 'sound' does not exist on type 'Animal'

Related Errors

  • TS2304: Cannot find name — identifier not in scope at all.
  • TS2345: Argument of type ‘X’ is not assignable — type mismatch in function calls.
  • TS2741: Property is missing in type — missing required properties on object literal.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2345 — Argument Type Not Assignable
item.description:
TS2345 — Argument of type ‘X’ is not assignable

TS2345 occurs when you pass an argument to a function that doesn’t match the expected parameter type. This is one of the most common TypeScript errors and covers a wide range of type incompatibilities.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Wrong primitive type
function greet(name: string) {}
greet(42); // TS2345: Argument of type 'number' is not assignable to parameter of type 'string'

// Cause 2: Missing required properties in object argument
function createUser(user: { name: string; age: number }) {}
createUser({ name: "Alice" }); // TS2345: Property 'age' is missing

// Cause 3: Array type mismatch
function processItems(items: number[]) {}
processItems(["a", "b"]); // TS2345: Type 'string[]' not assignable to 'number[]'

// Cause 4: Nullable argument to non-nullable parameter
function handle(input: string) {}
handle(null); // TS2345 if strictNullChecks is on

Solutions

Fix 1: Match the expected argument type

// Wrong
function greet(name: string) {}
greet(42);

// Correct
greet("Alice");

Fix 2: Provide all required properties

// Wrong
function createUser(user: { name: string; age: number }) {}
createUser({ name: "Alice" });

// Correct
createUser({ name: "Alice", age: 30 });

Fix 3: Cast or convert arguments to the expected type

function processItems(items: number[]) {}
processItems(["1", "2"].map(Number)); // Convert strings to numbers

Fix 4: Use union types to accept multiple types

function handle(input: string | null) {
 if (input === null) return;
 // input is string here
}
handle(null); // OK

Examples

// Example 1: Incompatible object literal
interface Config {
 host: string;
 port: number;
}
function startServer(config: Config) {}
startServer({ host: "localhost" }); // TS2345

// Example 2: Array vs single value
function log(items: string[]) {}
log("hello"); // TS2345: 'string' is not assignable to 'string[]'

Related Errors

  • TS2322: Type is not assignable — assignment context rather than argument context.
  • TS2769: No overload matches this call — function overloads don’t match any signature.
  • TS2554: Expected X arguments, but got Y — wrong argument count.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2349 — This Expression Is Not Callable
item.description:
TS2349 — This expression is not callable

TS2349 occurs when you try to call a value as a function using (), but its type doesn’t have a call signature. This happens when a variable holds a non-function value or when a type is not callable.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Calling a non-function value
const name = "Alice";
name(); // TS2349: This expression is not callable

// Cause 2: Calling an object as a function
const obj = { greet: "hello" };
obj(); // TS2349

// Cause 3: Calling a class instance as a function
class Greeter {
 greet() { return "hello"; }
}
const g = new Greeter();
g(); // TS2349

// Cause 4: Property that resolves to a non-function
interface Service {
 handler: string | (() => void);
}
function invoke(service: Service) {
 service.handler(); // TS2349: handler might be a string
}

Solutions

Fix 1: Verify the value is actually a function

// Wrong
const name = "Alice";
name();

// Correct
const greet = () => "Hello, Alice!";
greet();

Fix 2: Use a type guard before calling

interface Service {
 handler: string | (() => void);
}

function invoke(service: Service) {
 if (typeof service.handler === "function") {
 service.handler(); // OK
 }
}

Fix 3: Call the correct method

// Wrong
const obj = { greet: "hello" };
obj();

// Correct
const obj = { greet: () => "hello" };
obj.greet();

Fix 4: Ensure proper function assignment

// Wrong — property is not a function
const config = {
 init: false,
};
config.init(); // TS2349

// Correct
const config = {
 init: () => console.log("initialized"),
};
config.init();

Examples

// Example 1: Module default export is not a function
import myModule from "./my-module";
myModule(); // TS2349 if default export is an object

// Example 2: Array methods confused with call
const arr = [1, 2, 3];
arr(); // TS2349: arrays are not callable

Related Errors

  • TS2351: This expression is not constructable — trying to use new on non-constructable.
  • TS2688: Type has no matching call signature — callable but wrong arguments.
  • TS2345: Argument type not assignable — callable but wrong argument types.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2351 — This Expression Is Not Constructable
item.description:
TS2351 — This expression is not constructable

TS2351 occurs when you try to use new on a value whose type doesn’t have a construct signature. This happens when calling new on a function that isn’t a constructor, an object, or a primitive.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Using 'new' on a regular function
function greet(name: string) {
 return `Hello, ${name}`;
}
const g = new greet("Alice"); // TS2351: not constructable

// Cause 2: Using 'new' on an arrow function
const create = () => ({ x: 1 });
const obj = new create(); // TS2351

// Cause 3: Using 'new' on a module default export that isn't a class
import utils from "./utils";
const u = new utils(); // TS2351

// Cause 4: Using 'new' on an interface
interface Factory {
 create(): Widget;
}
const f = new Factory(); // TS2351: interfaces aren't values

Solutions

Fix 1: Use a class with a constructor

// Wrong — regular function
function Greeter(name: string) {
 this.name = name;
}
new Greeter("Alice"); // TS2351

// Correct — use a class
class Greeter {
 name: string;
 constructor(name: string) {
 this.name = name;
 }
}
new Greeter("Alice"); // OK

Fix 2: Use factory function without ’new'

// Wrong
const create = () => ({ x: 1 });
const obj = new create();

// Correct — call without 'new'
const obj = create();

Fix 3: Import the class correctly

// Wrong — importing non-class default export
import utils from "./utils";
new utils();

// Correct — import a class
import { MyClass } from "./my-class";
new MyClass();

Fix 4: Define a construct signature for the type

interface Constructor {
 new (name: string): { name: string };
}

function createInstance(Ctor: Constructor, name: string) {
 return new Ctor(name); // OK
}

Examples

// Example 1: Using 'new' on a function stored in a variable
const initialize = function () {};
const app = new initialize(); // TS2351

// Example 2: Using 'new' on a namespace
namespace Utils {
 export function helper() {}
}
new Utils(); // TS2351

Related Errors

  • TS2349: This expression is not callable — trying to call () on non-callable.
  • TS2688: Type has no matching call signature — callable but wrong arguments.
  • TS2542: Cannot read property of type — property access issues.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2352 — Conversion May Be a Mistake
item.description:
TS2352 — Conversion of type ‘X’ to type ‘Y’ may be a mistake

TS2352 occurs when you use a type assertion (as or angle brackets) to convert between two unrelated types. TypeScript warns that the assertion might be incorrect because the types don’t overlap structurally. This is stricter in --strict mode.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Asserting between unrelated types
const value: string = "hello";
const num: number = value as number; // TS2352: Conversion may be a mistake

// Cause 2: Asserting object literals to incompatible types
interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}
const data = { x: 1, y: 2 };
const user = data as User; // TS2352: types don't overlap

// Cause 3: Using angle bracket assertion
const value = "hello";
const num = <number>value; // TS2352

// Cause 4: Assertion between primitive types
let a: boolean = true;
let b: number = a as number; // TS2352

Solutions

Fix 1: Use a more appropriate assertion

// Wrong
const value: string = "123";
const num: number = value as number; // TS2352

// Correct — use actual conversion
const num: number = Number(value);
const num2: number = parseInt(value, 10);

Fix 2: Use a type guard instead of assertion

function isUser(obj: unknown): obj is User {
 return (
 typeof obj === "object" &&
 obj !== null &&
 "name" in obj &&
 "age" in obj
 );
}

const data = { name: "Alice", age: 30 };
if (isUser(data)) {
 // data is User
}

Fix 3: Use unknown as intermediate type

// Wrong
const value: string = "hello";
const num: number = value as number;

// Correct — go through unknown
const intermediate: unknown = value;
// Then narrow or convert properly

Fix 4: Ensure structural compatibility

// If types need to be compatible, ensure they share structure
interface Source {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}
interface Target {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}
const source: Source = { name: "Alice", age: 30 };
const target: Target = source as Target; // OK — structural match

Examples

// Example 1: API response typed incorrectly
interface ApiResponse {
 data: { id: number; name: string };
}
const response = await fetch("/api");
const json = await response.json();
const result = json as ApiResponse; // TS2352 — json is 'any'

// Example 2: DOM element type mismatch
const div = document.getElementById("app");
const canvas = div as HTMLCanvasElement; // TS2352 if strict

Related Errors

  • TS2322: Type is not assignable — direct assignment without assertion.
  • TS2345: Argument type not assignable — function argument issues.
  • TS18046: ‘X’ is of type ‘unknown’ — needs type narrowing.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2367 — Comparison Appears to Be Unintentional
item.description:
TS2367 — This comparison appears to be unintentional

TS2367 occurs when a comparison is always true or always false because the two sides of the comparison have types with no overlap. TypeScript flags these as likely bugs — you’re comparing values that can never be equal (or always are).

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Comparing a type with a value it can never hold
let status: "active" | "inactive";
if (status === "deleted") { // TS2367: This comparison appears to be unintentional
 // "deleted" is not in the union
}

// Cause 2: Comparing a string literal union with an invalid value
type Color = "red" | "green" | "blue";
const isRed = color === "yellow"; // TS2367

// Cause 3: Comparing a number enum with a non-existent member
enum Direction { Up, Down }
const isLeft = Direction.Up === 2; // TS2367: Comparison always false

// Cause 4: Comparing 'never' type with anything
function exhaustive(x: never) {
 if (x === "something") {} // TS2367
}

Solutions

Fix 1: Use valid values from the union

// Wrong
let status: "active" | "inactive";
if (status === "deleted") {} // TS2367

// Correct
if (status === "active") {} // Valid comparison

Fix 2: Widen the type to accept the comparison value

// Wrong
let status: "active" | "inactive";

// Correct — if "deleted" is a valid state, add it to the union
let status: "active" | "inactive" | "deleted";
if (status === "deleted") {} // OK

Fix 3: Remove impossible branches

type Fruit = "apple" | "banana";

function describe(fruit: Fruit) {
 if (fruit === "orange") {
 // This branch is unreachable — TS2367
 return "This never happens";
 }
 return fruit; // Just handle valid cases
}

Fix 4: Use a type guard function for runtime checks

function isString(value: unknown): value is string {
 return typeof value === "string";
}

let x: number | string;
if (isString(x)) {
 // x is string here
}

Examples

// Example 1: Exhaustive switch without proper typing
type Shape = "circle" | "square";
function area(shape: Shape) {
 switch (shape) {
 case "circle": return Math.PI;
 case "square": return 1;
 case "triangle": return 0; // TS2367
 }
}

// Example 2: Comparing different enum types
enum A { X, Y }
enum B { X, Y }
const eq = A.X === B.X; // TS2367: A.X and B.X are different types

Related Errors

  • TS2366: A function whose declared type is neither ‘undefined’ nor ‘void’ must return a value — related to unreachable code.
  • TS2345: Argument of type ‘X’ is not assignable — passing invalid values to functions.
  • TS18046: ‘X’ is of type ‘unknown’ — untyped values need narrowing.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2464 — Class Can Only Implement an Identifier
item.description:
TS2464 — A class can only implement an identifier/qualified-name

TS2464 occurs when you use implements with an expression that isn’t a valid identifier or qualified name. You can only implement named interfaces or classes, not object literals, unions, or computed values.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Implementing an object literal
class MyClass implements { name: string; age: number } { // TS2464
 name = "";
 age = 0;
}

// Cause 2: Implementing a union type
interface A { a: string; }
interface B { b: string; }
class MyClass implements A | B { // TS2464
 a = "";
}

// Cause 3: Implementing an expression
const myInterface = { x: number };
class MyClass implements myInterface {} // TS2464

// Cause 4: Implementing an inline type without a name
class MyClass implements { id: number } {} // TS2464

Solutions

Fix 1: Define an interface and implement it

// Wrong
class MyClass implements { name: string } {}

// Correct
interface Named {
 name: string;
}
class MyClass implements Named {
 name = "";
}

Fix 2: Only implement one interface (not a union)

// Wrong
class MyClass implements A | B {}

// Correct — implement both separately
class MyClass implements A, B {
 a = "";
 b = "";
}

Fix 3: Use interface reference instead of inline type

// Wrong
const myInterface = { x: number };
class MyClass implements myInterface {}

// Correct
interface HasX {
 x: number;
}
class MyClass implements HasX {
 x = 0;
}

Fix 4: Implement named type aliases

type Printable = { print(): void };
class MyClass implements Printable {
 print() { console.log("printed"); }
}

Examples

// Example 1: Trying to implement a generic type without instantiation
interface Container<T> { value: T; }
class MyClass implements Container { // TS2464 — missing type argument
 value = "";
}

// Fix:
class MyClass implements Container<string> {
 value = "";
}

// Example 2: Implementing a mapped type
type Options = { [key: string]: string };
class Config implements Options {} // TS2464 — can't implement mapped type directly

Related Errors

  • TS2420: Class incorrectly implements interface — implements but missing members.
  • TS2339: Property does not exist on type — accessing non-existent properties.
  • TS2741: Property is missing in type — missing required properties.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2531 — Object Is Possibly 'null'
item.description:
TS2531 — Object is possibly ’null'

TS2531 occurs when you access a property or call a method on an object that could be null. This is enforced when strictNullChecks is enabled in your tsconfig, which is the default in modern TypeScript projects.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: DOM query that might return null
const el = document.getElementById("app");
el.innerHTML = "Hello"; // TS2531: Object is possibly 'null'

// Cause 2: Function parameter that accepts null
function process(value: string | null) {
 console.log(value.length); // TS2531
}

// Cause 3: Array.find() returns undefined
const items = [1, 2, 3];
const found = items.find(x => x === 99);
console.log(found.toFixed()); // TS2531 (find returns T | undefined)

// Cause 4: Optional property access without null check
interface Config {
 host?: string;
}
function getHost(config: Config) {
 return config.host.toUpperCase(); // TS2531
}

Solutions

Fix 1: Add a null check before accessing properties

// Wrong
const el = document.getElementById("app");
el.innerHTML = "Hello";

// Correct
const el = document.getElementById("app");
if (el) {
 el.innerHTML = "Hello";
}

Fix 2: Use optional chaining

// Wrong
const el = document.getElementById("app");
el.innerHTML = "Hello";

// Correct
const el = document.getElementById("app");
el?.innerHTML = "Hello";

Fix 3: Use non-null assertion when you’re sure

// Use ! only when you're certain the value is not null
const el = document.getElementById("app")!;
el.innerHTML = "Hello";

Fix 4: Use type narrowing in function parameters

function process(value: string | null) {
 if (value === null) {
 console.log("No value provided");
 return;
 }
 console.log(value.length); // OK — value is string
}

Examples

// Example 1: Document.querySelector returns Element | null
const btn = document.querySelector("button");
btn.addEventListener("click", handler); // TS2531

// Example 2: Object destructuring with optional values
interface Response {
 data?: { name: string };
}
function getName(res: Response) {
 return res.data.name; // TS2531: data is possibly undefined
}

Related Errors

  • TS2532: Object is possibly ‘undefined’ — similar but for undefined instead of null.
  • TS2322: Type is not assignable — broader type assignability issues.
  • TS18046: ‘X’ is of type ‘unknown’ — untyped values need narrowing.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2532 — Object Is Possibly 'undefined'
item.description:
TS2532 — Object is possibly ‘undefined’

TS2532 occurs when you access a property or call a method on a value that could be undefined. With strictNullChecks enabled, TypeScript requires you to verify that a value exists before using it.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Array element access without bounds checking
const arr: number[] = [1, 2, 3];
const value = arr[10];
value.toFixed(); // TS2532: Object is possibly 'undefined'

// Cause 2: Object property access on optional property
interface Config {
 database?: { host: string };
}
function getHost(config: Config) {
 return config.database.host; // TS2532: database is possibly undefined
}

// Cause 3: Map.get() returns V | undefined
const map = new Map<string, number>();
const val = map.get("key");
val.toFixed(); // TS2532

// Cause 4: Function return type includes undefined
function findUser(id: number): User | undefined {
 return users.find(u => u.id === id);
}
const user = findUser(1);
console.log(user.name); // TS2532

Solutions

Fix 1: Use optional chaining

// Wrong
const val = map.get("key");
val.toFixed();

// Correct
const val = map.get("key");
val?.toFixed();

Fix 2: Add a truthiness check

// Wrong
const user = findUser(1);
console.log(user.name);

// Correct
const user = findUser(1);
if (user) {
 console.log(user.name);
}

Fix 3: Use default values with nullish coalescing

const val = map.get("key") ?? 0;
val.toFixed(); // OK — val is number

Fix 4: Use non-null assertion when certain

// Use ! only when you're sure the value exists
const val = map.get("key")!;
val.toFixed();

Examples

// Example 1: Nested optional properties
interface Company {
 ceo?: {
 name?: string;
 };
}
function getCEOName(company: Company) {
 return company.ceo.name; // TS2532: ceo and name are possibly undefined
}
// Fix: company.ceo?.name

// Example 2: Promise.then with possible undefined
Promise.resolve(42).then(result => {
 result.toFixed(); // OK — result is number
});

Related Errors

  • TS2531: Object is possibly ’null’ — similar but for null instead of undefined.
  • TS2322: Type is not assignable — broader type assignability.
  • TS2741: Property is missing in type — related to object shape mismatches.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2540 — Cannot Assign to Read-Only Property
item.description:
TS2540 — Cannot assign to ‘X’ because it is a read-only property

TS2540 occurs when you try to modify a value that’s marked as readonly, const, or comes from a Readonly<T> type. TypeScript prevents mutation of these values at compile time.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Assigning to a const variable
const x = 10;
x = 20; // TS2540: Cannot assign to 'x' because it is a constant

// Cause 2: Modifying a readonly property
interface Config {
 readonly host: string;
}
const config: Config = { host: "localhost" };
config.host = "other.com"; // TS2540

// Cause 3: Modifying a readonly array
const arr: readonly number[] = [1, 2, 3];
arr.push(4); // TS2540

// Cause 4: Modifying a property in Readonly<T>
interface User {
 name: string;
}
const user: Readonly<User> = { name: "Alice" };
user.name = "Bob"; // TS2540

Solutions

Fix 1: Use ’let’ instead of ‘const’ for mutable variables

// Wrong
const x = 10;
x = 20; // TS2540

// Correct
let x = 10;
x = 20; // OK

Fix 2: Remove ‘readonly’ from the property

// Wrong
interface Config {
 readonly host: string;
}

// Correct — if mutation is intended
interface Config {
 host: string;
}

Fix 3: Create a mutable copy

// Wrong
const arr: readonly number[] = [1, 2, 3];
arr.push(4); // TS2540

// Correct
const arr: readonly number[] = [1, 2, 3];
const mutableArr = [...arr]; // Spread into mutable array
mutableArr.push(4);

Fix 4: Use a type assertion to bypass readonly (use carefully)

// Use only when you're certain mutation is safe
const config: Readonly<Config> = { host: "localhost" };
(config as Config).host = "other.com"; // Bypasses readonly

Examples

// Example 1: Readonly class property
class ApiClient {
 readonly baseUrl = "https://api.example.com";
}
const client = new ApiClient();
client.baseUrl = "https://other.com"; // TS2540

// Example 2: Object.freeze returns Readonly
const frozen = Object.freeze({ x: 1, y: 2 });
frozen.x = 10; // TS2540

// Example 3: Tuple assignment
const tuple: readonly [string, number] = ["hello", 42];
tuple[0] = "world"; // TS2540

Related Errors

  • TS2542: Cannot read property of type — readonly prevents reading in some contexts.
  • TS2588: Cannot assign to ‘const’ variable — specific to const variables.
  • TS2322: Type is not assignable — broader type issues.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2542 — Cannot Read Property of Type
item.description:
TS2542 — Cannot read property ‘X’ of type ‘Y’

TS2542 occurs when you try to read a property from a type that doesn’t allow property access, typically with Readonly<T> types or when accessing properties on types that lack index signatures. This is less common than TS2540 and usually involves specific readonly constraints.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Accessing property on wrong type
interface ReadonlyUser {
 readonly name: string;
 readonly age: number;
}
const user: ReadonlyUser = { name: "Alice", age: 30 };
const data: ReadonlyUser = user;
// TS2542 if you try to access data on a type that doesn't expose it

// Cause 2: Indexed access on a readonly tuple
const tuple: readonly [string, number] = ["hello", 42];
const val: string = tuple[0]; // OK
tuple[0] = "world"; // TS2540 (write), not TS2542

// Cause 3: Using 'in' operator on type without property
type Empty = {};
const x: Empty = {};
"prop" in x; // No error, but...

// Cause 4: Readonly mapped type access
type ReadonlyObj = Readonly<{ x: number; y: number }>;
const obj: ReadonlyObj = { x: 1, y: 2 };

Solutions

Fix 1: Use the correct type that allows property access

// If you need to read properties, ensure the type exposes them
interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}
const user: User = { name: "Alice", age: 30 };
console.log(user.name); // OK

Fix 2: Use a mutable type when mutation is needed

// Wrong — readonly prevents writes
type ReadonlyUser = Readonly<{ name: string }>;

// Correct — use mutable type for mutable operations
type User = { name: string };

Fix 3: Cast to a type with the needed property

interface Base {
 id: number;
}
interface Extended extends Base {
 name: string;
}
const obj: Base = { id: 1 };
(obj as Extended).name; // Access via assertion

Fix 4: Use type narrowing for conditional access

function getValue(obj: unknown) {
