 if (typeof obj === "object" && obj !== null && "name" in obj) {
 return (obj as { name: string }).name; // Safe access
 }
}

Examples

// Example 1: Readonly array methods
const arr: readonly number[] = [1, 2, 3];
arr.filter(x => x > 1); // OK — read-only methods allowed
arr.sort(); // TS2542 — mutating method not allowed on readonly array

// Example 2: Frozen object
const frozen = Object.freeze({ config: { debug: true } });
frozen.config = { debug: false }; // TS2542 / TS2540

Related Errors

  • TS2540: Cannot assign to read-only property — write access on readonly.
  • TS2339: Property does not exist on type — property not declared.
  • TS2588: Cannot assign to ‘const’ variable — const assignment.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2551 — Property Does Not Exist (With Suggestions)
item.description:
TS2551 — Property ‘X’ does not exist (did you mean…)

TS2551 is a variant of TS2339 where TypeScript detects that the property doesn’t exist but finds a similarly-named property on the type. It usually includes a “did you mean…” suggestion, making it easier to fix typos.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Typo in property name
interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}
const user: User = { name: "Alice", age: 30 };
console.log(user.nme); // TS2551: Property 'nme' does not exist. Did you mean 'name'?

// Cause 2: Typo in method name
const arr = [1, 2, 3];
arr.filer(x => x > 1); // TS2551: Did you mean 'filter'?

// Cause 3: Wrong casing (camelCase vs PascalCase)
interface HttpResponse {
 statusCode: number;
}
const res: HttpResponse = { statusCode: 200 };
console.log(res.StatusCode); // TS2551: Did you mean 'statusCode'?

// Cause 4: Accessing property on wrong object
interface Car { make: string; }
interface Truck { bed: boolean; }
function processVehicle(v: Car) {
 console.log(v.bed); // TS2551: Property 'bed' does not exist on type 'Car'
}

Solutions

Fix 1: Use the suggested property name

// Wrong
console.log(user.nme);

// Correct — follow the "did you mean" suggestion
console.log(user.name);

Fix 2: Check casing conventions

// Wrong — PascalCase
console.log(res.StatusCode);

// Correct — camelCase
console.log(res.statusCode);

Fix 3: Verify you’re using the right object type

interface Car { make: string; }
interface Truck { bed: boolean; }

// If you need truck properties, accept Truck type
function processVehicle(v: Car | Truck) {
 if ("bed" in v) {
 console.log(v.bed); // OK — v is Truck
 }
}

Fix 4: Use IDE autocompletion to discover correct names

Most modern IDEs show available properties as you type, preventing these typos before compilation.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2552 — Cannot Find Name, Did You Mean
item.description:
TS2552 — Cannot find name ‘X’, did you mean ‘Y’

TS2552 is an enhanced version of TS2304 where TypeScript not only reports that a name isn’t found, but also suggests a similar name that might be what you intended. This makes fixing typos much easier.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Typo in variable name
let userName = "Alice";
console.log(useName); // TS2552: Cannot find name 'useName'. Did you mean 'userName'?

// Cause 2: Typo in function name
function calculateTotal(items: number[]) {}
calculatTotal([1, 2, 3]); // TS2552: Did you mean 'calculateTotal'?

// Cause 3: Missing import with close alternative
import { UserService } from "./services";
const svc = new UsersService(); // TS2552: Did you mean 'UserService'?

// Cause 4: Wrong casing
const myArray = [1, 2, 3];
myarray.length; // TS2552: Did you mean 'myArray'?

Solutions

Fix 1: Use the suggested name

// Wrong
let userName = "Alice";
console.log(useName); // TS2552

// Correct — follow the suggestion
console.log(userName);

Fix 2: Import the correct name

// Wrong
import { UserService } from "./services";
const svc = new UsersService(); // TS2552

// Correct
const svc = new UserService();

Fix 3: Check for case sensitivity

// Wrong — lowercase 'r'
const result = myarray.reduce((a, b) => a + b, 0);

// Correct — PascalCase or camelCase as defined
const result = myArray.reduce((a, b) => a + b, 0);

Fix 4: Ensure the identifier is in scope

// Wrong — using variable before it's defined
console.log(myVar);
const myVar = 10; // TS2552

// Correct
const myVar = 10;
console.log(myVar);

Examples

// Example 1: DOM API typo
const canvas = document.getElementById("myCanvas");
const ctx = canvas.getContex("2d"); // TS2552: Did you mean 'getContext'?

// Example 2: Array method typo
const nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5];
const evens = nums.fliter(x => x % 2 === 0); // TS2552: Did you mean 'filter'?

// Example 3: Class method typo
class Calculator {
 add(a: number, b: number) { return a + b; }
}
const calc = new Calculator();
calc.addd(1, 2); // TS2552: Did you mean 'add'?

Related Errors

  • TS2304: Cannot find name — no suggestion provided.
  • TS2551: Property does not exist — property-level suggestion.
  • TS1435: Unknown keyword or identifier — unrecognized keyword.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2554 — Expected X Arguments But Got Y
item.description:
TS2554 — Expected X arguments, but got Y

TS2554 occurs when you call a function with a different number of arguments than its signature expects. Unlike JavaScript, TypeScript enforces exact argument counts based on the function’s type declaration.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Too few arguments
function greet(name: string, greeting: string) {}
greet("Alice"); // TS2554: Expected 2 arguments, but got 1

// Cause 2: Too many arguments
function add(a: number, b: number) {}
add(1, 2, 3); // TS2554: Expected 2 arguments, but got 3

// Cause 3: Calling without required arguments
function fetchData(url: string, callback: Function) {}
fetchData(); // TS2554: Expected 2 arguments, but got 0

// Cause 4: Spread operator passing wrong count
function log(a: string, b: string) {}
const args = ["hello"];
log(...args); // TS2554: Expected 2 arguments, but got 1

Solutions

Fix 1: Provide the correct number of arguments

// Wrong
function greet(name: string, greeting: string) {}
greet("Alice");

// Correct
greet("Alice", "Hello");

Fix 2: Make parameters optional if they aren’t always needed

// Wrong — greeting should be optional
function greet(name: string, greeting: string) {}

// Correct
function greet(name: string, greeting?: string) {
 const g = greeting ?? "Hello";
 return `${g}, ${name}!`;
}
greet("Alice"); // OK
greet("Alice", "Hi"); // OK

Fix 3: Use rest parameters for variable argument count

// Wrong — fixed 2 args
function log(a: string, b: string) {}

// Correct — variable args
function log(...args: string[]) {}
log("hello"); // OK
log("hello", "world"); // OK

Fix 4: Remove extra arguments

// Wrong
add(1, 2, 3);

// Correct
add(1, 2);

Examples

// Example 1: Callback with wrong arity
function onComplete(success: boolean, message: string) {}
onComplete(true); // TS2554: Missing second argument

// Example 2: Spread with wrong count
const coords = [10, 20, 30] as const;
function point2D(x: number, y: number) {}
point2D(...coords); // TS2554: Expected 2, but got 3

Related Errors

  • TS2345: Argument type not assignable — right count, wrong types.
  • TS2769: No overload matches this call — no overload signature matches.
  • TS2551: Property does not exist — might mean you’re calling a non-existent method.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2588 — Cannot Assign to 'const' Variable
item.description:
TS2588 — Cannot assign to ‘const’ variable

TS2588 occurs when you try to reassign a variable declared with const. Unlike let, const variables cannot be reassigned after initialization. This is a fundamental JavaScript/TypeScript rule enforced at compile time.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Reassigning a const variable
const x = 10;
x = 20; // TS2588: Cannot assign to 'x' because it is a constant

// Cause 2: Trying to reassign in a loop
const counter = 0;
for (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
 counter++; // TS2588
}

// Cause 3: Reassigning a function parameter (const in destructuring)
function process({ id, name }: { id: number; name: string }) {
 id = 0; // TS2588: 'id' is a const
}

// Cause 4: Increment/decrement operator on const
const count = 0;
count++; // TS2588

Solutions

Fix 1: Use ’let’ for variables that need reassignment

// Wrong
const x = 10;
x = 20; // TS2588

// Correct
let x = 10;
x = 20; // OK

Fix 2: Use ’let’ for counters

// Wrong
const counter = 0;
for (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
 counter++; // TS2588
}

// Correct
let counter = 0;
for (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
 counter++;
}

Fix 3: Use a new variable instead of reassignment

// Wrong
const count = 0;
count++;

// Correct — create a new value
const count = 0;
const newCount = count + 1;

Fix 4: Use mutable objects with const

// Const prevents reassignment, not mutation
const arr = [1, 2, 3];
arr.push(4); // OK — mutation is allowed
arr = [5, 6, 7]; // TS2588 — reassignment is not

// If you need to "replace" the array
const arr2 = [...arr, 4]; // New array, same reference

Examples

// Example 1: React state with const
function Counter() {
 const [count, setCount] = useState(0);
 // count++; // TS2588 — can't reassign const
 setCount(count + 1); // Correct — use setter
}

// Example 2: Trying to swap values
let a = 1;
let b = 2;
const temp = a;
a = b;
b = temp; // OK — temp is const but we're not reassigning it
// But: temp = 5; // TS2588

// Example 3: Destructured const
const [first, second] = [1, 2];
// first = 10; // TS2588

Related Errors

  • TS2540: Cannot assign to read-only property — readonly object properties.
  • TS2542: Cannot read property of type — readonly access issues.
  • TS2322: Type is not assignable — broader type assignment issues.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2683 — 'this' Implicitly Has Type 'any'
item.description:
TS2683 — ’this’ implicitly has type ‘any’

TS2683 occurs when noImplicitThis is enabled (part of strict mode) and TypeScript can’t determine the type of this in a function. This commonly happens with standalone functions, callbacks, and untyped method references.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Standalone function without 'this' parameter
function greet() {
 console.log(this.name); // TS2683: 'this' implicitly has type 'any'
}

// Cause 2: Callback losing 'this' context
class Timer {
 seconds = 0;
 start() {
 setInterval(function () {
 this.seconds++; // TS2683: 'this' is 'any' inside callback
 }, 1000);
 }
}

// Cause 3: Passing method as callback without binding
class Logger {
 log() {
 console.log("logged");
 }
}
const fn = new Logger().log;
fn(); // TS2683: 'this' is 'any'

// Cause 4: Object literal with untyped 'this'
const obj = {
 value: 10,
 getValue() {
 return this.value; // TS2683 if noImplicitThis is on
 }
};

Solutions

Fix 1: Add a ’this’ parameter to the function

// Wrong
function greet() {
 console.log(this.name);
}

// Correct
interface ThisContext {
 name: string;
}
function greet(this: ThisContext) {
 console.log(this.name);
}

Fix 2: Use arrow functions to preserve ’this'

// Wrong
class Timer {
 seconds = 0;
 start() {
 setInterval(function () {
 this.seconds++;
 }, 1000);
 }
}

// Correct — arrow function captures 'this' lexically
class Timer {
 seconds = 0;
 start() {
 setInterval(() => {
 this.seconds++;
 }, 1000);
 }
}

Fix 3: Use .bind() when passing methods as callbacks

class Logger {
 log() {
 console.log("logged");
 }
}
const logger = new Logger();
const fn = logger.log.bind(logger);
fn();

Fix 4: Type ’this’ in arrow function properties

const obj = {
 value: 10,
 getValue: function (this: { value: number }) {
 return this.value;
 }
};

Examples

// Example 1: jQuery-style callback
$(".button").on("click", function () {
 $(this).addClass("active"); // TS2683: 'this' is HTMLElement, not any
 // Fix: add 'this: HTMLElement' parameter
});

// Example 2: Event handler
document.addEventListener("click", function (e) {
 console.log(this.id); // TS2683: 'this' is Document, not HTMLElement
});

Related Errors

  • TS2684: The ’this’ context of type ‘X’ is not assignable — ’this’ type mismatch.
  • TS7006: Parameter implicitly has ‘any’ type — other implicit any issues.
  • TS7031: Binding element implicitly has ‘any’ type — destructured implicit any.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2684 — The 'this' Context Type Is Not Assignable
item.description:
TS2684 — The ’this’ context of type ‘X’ is not assignable

TS2684 occurs when the actual this context in a function doesn’t match the declared this parameter type. This is stricter than TS2683 — TypeScript knows what this is, but it doesn’t match what the function expects.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: 'this' parameter type doesn't match the object
interface Logger {
 log: (msg: string) => void;
 prefix: string;
}
function doLog(this: Logger, msg: string) {
 console.log(this.prefix + msg);
}
const obj = { prefix: "[APP]", log: console.log };
doLog.call(obj, "hello"); // TS2684: 'this' context mismatch

// Cause 2: Method called on wrong object type
class Animal {
 speak() {}
}
class Robot {
 code() {}
}
const robot = new Robot();
const speak = Animal.prototype.speak;
speak.call(robot); // TS2684: Robot is not Animal

// Cause 3: Arrow function captures wrong 'this'
class Outer {
 value = 10;
 method() {
 const inner = {
 value: 20,
 getThis: () => this.value // Captures Outer's 'this', not inner's
 };
 }
}

// Cause 4: Calling method with incompatible receiver
interface HasId {
 id: number;
}
function getId(this: HasId) {
 return this.id;
}
getId.call({ name: "Alice" }); // TS2684: missing 'id'

Solutions

Fix 1: Ensure the object matches the ’this’ parameter type

interface Logger {
 log: (msg: string) => void;
 prefix: string;
}

// Wrong — obj doesn't fully match Logger
const obj = { prefix: "[APP]", log: console.log };

// Correct — ensure full type match
const obj: Logger = { prefix: "[APP]", log: console.log };
doLog.call(obj, "hello");

Fix 2: Use .bind() with the correct type

class Animal {
 speak() { return "speaking"; }
}
const animal = new Animal();
const speak = animal.speak.bind(animal);
speak(); // OK

Fix 3: Use arrow functions to capture the right ’this’

class Outer {
 value = 10;
 method() {
 // Arrow function captures Outer's this
 const getOuterValue = () => this.value;
 return getOuterValue();
 }
}

Fix 4: Match ’this’ parameter to actual context

interface HasId {
 id: number;
}

// Wrong — calling with object that doesn't have 'id'
getId.call({ name: "Alice" });

// Correct — pass object with required shape
getId.call({ id: 1, name: "Alice" });

Examples

// Example 1: React class component
class MyComponent {
 state = { count: 0 };
 handleClick() {
 this.setState({ count: this.state.count + 1 }); // 'this' must be MyComponent
 }
}

// Example 2: forEach callback with 'this' parameter
const arr = [1, 2, 3];
arr.forEach(function (item) {
 console.log(this); // 'this' must match declared type
}, someObject);

Related Errors

  • TS2683: ’this’ implicitly has type ‘any’ — ’this’ has no type at all.
  • TS2322: Type is not assignable — broader assignability.
  • TS2345: Argument type not assignable — function argument mismatches.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2688 — Type Has No Matching Call Signature
item.description:
TS2688 — Type ‘X’ has no matching call signature

TS2688 occurs when you try to call a value as a function, but its type doesn’t have a call signature that matches the arguments provided. This differs from TS2349 (not callable at all) — here the type might be callable, but not with your specific arguments.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Calling with wrong argument types
type Callback = (x: number) => void;
const fn: Callback = (x) => console.log(x);
fn("hello"); // TS2688: string is not assignable to number

// Cause 2: Calling union type that isn't callable in all cases
type MaybeFn = (() => void) | null;
const value: MaybeFn = null;
value(); // TS2688: 'null' has no call signature

// Cause 3: Calling a type that isn't a function
type Config = { host: string; port: number };
const config: Config = { host: "localhost", port: 3000 };
config(); // TS2688: Config has no call signature

// Cause 4: Generic type without call signature
type Container<T> = { value: T };
const container: Container<number> = { value: 42 };
container(); // TS2688

Solutions

Fix 1: Provide arguments that match the call signature

// Wrong
type Callback = (x: number) => void;
const fn: Callback = (x) => console.log(x);
fn("hello"); // TS2688

// Correct
fn(42);

Fix 2: Narrow the type before calling

// Wrong
type MaybeFn = (() => void) | null;
const value: MaybeFn = null;
value(); // TS2688

// Correct
if (value) {
 value(); // OK — value is () => void
}

Fix 3: Use a type guard or typeof check

type ConfigOrFunction = Config | (() => void);
const value: ConfigOrFunction = getSomething();

if (typeof value === "function") {
 value(); // OK — TypeScript knows it's callable
}

Fix 4: Ensure the type has a call signature

// Wrong
type Config = { host: string };
const config: Config = { host: "localhost" };
config(); // TS2688

// Correct — define a callable interface
interface Factory {
 (): Config;
 create(): Config;
}

Examples

// Example 1: Array method type mismatch
const nums: number[] = [1, 2, 3];
nums.includes("1"); // TS2688: string not assignable to number

// Example 2: Promise.resolve type
const p: Promise<number> = Promise.resolve(42);
p.then("not a function"); // TS2688

Related Errors

  • TS2349: This expression is not callable — value has no call signature at all.
  • TS2345: Argument type not assignable — argument type mismatch.
  • TS2769: No overload matches this call — overload resolution failure.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2740 — Type Is Missing Properties
item.description:
TS2740 — Type ‘X’ is missing the following properties

TS2740 occurs when an object literal or value doesn’t have all the required properties that the target type demands. This is closely related to TS2741 but typically involves more complex type structures or when assigning to a variable rather than a function parameter.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Missing properties in object literal assignment
interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
 email: string;
}
const user: User = { name: "Alice" }; // TS2740: missing 'age' and 'email'

// Cause 2: Missing properties from intersection type
interface A { a: string; }
interface B { b: number; }
type AB = A & B;
const ab: AB = { a: "hello" }; // TS2740: missing 'b'

// Cause 3: Class not implementing all interface members
interface Repository {
 find(id: number): any;
 save(entity: any): void;
 delete(id: number): void;
}
class SimpleRepo implements Repository {
 find(id: number) { return null; } // TS2740: missing 'save' and 'delete'
}

// Cause 4: Missing properties from generic constraint
interface Form<T> {
 values: T;
 errors: Record<keyof T, string>;
}
const form: Form<{ name: string }> = {
 values: { name: "" }
 // TS2740: missing 'errors'
};

Solutions

Fix 1: Add all missing properties

// Wrong
const user: User = { name: "Alice" };

// Correct
const user: User = { name: "Alice", age: 30, email: "alice@example.com" };

Fix 2: Use Partial if properties are optional

// Wrong
interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}
const user: User = { name: "Alice" }; // TS2740

// Correct — use Partial for optional
const user: Partial<User> = { name: "Alice" };

Fix 3: Implement all interface members in classes

// Wrong
class SimpleRepo implements Repository {
 find(id: number) { return null; }
}

// Correct
class SimpleRepo implements Repository {
 find(id: number) { return null; }
 save(entity: any) { /* ... */ }
 delete(id: number) { /* ... */ }
}

Fix 4: Provide defaults or spread missing properties

const defaults = { age: 0, email: "" };
const user: User = { ...defaults, name: "Alice" };

Examples

// Example 1: React state type
interface State {
 count: number;
 loading: boolean;
 error: string | null;
}
this.setState({ count: 0 }); // TS2740: missing 'loading' and 'error'

// Fix: use Partial
this.setState({ count: 0 } as Partial<State>);

// Example 2: API response type
interface ApiResponse {
 status: number;
 data: unknown;
 headers: Record<string, string>;
}
const res: ApiResponse = { status: 200, data: {} }; // TS2740

Related Errors

  • TS2741: Property is missing in type — similar but for single property.
  • TS2322: Type is not assignable — broader assignability.
  • TS2339: Property does not exist on type — accessing non-existent properties.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2741 — Property Is Missing in Type
item.description:
TS2741 — Property ‘X’ is missing in type ‘Y’

TS2741 occurs when an object literal or value is missing required properties that the target type demands. This is a specific variant of TS2322 focused on missing properties in object shapes.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Missing required property in object literal
interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
 email: string;
}
const user: User = { name: "Alice", age: 30 }; // TS2741: Property 'email' is missing

// Cause 2: Missing property in function return
interface Config {
 host: string;
 port: number;
 debug: boolean;
}
function getConfig(): Config {
 return { host: "localhost", port: 3000 }; // TS2741: 'debug' is missing
}

// Cause 3: Missing property when implementing an interface
interface Serializable {
 serialize(): string;
 validate(): boolean;
}
class MyClass implements Serializable {
 serialize() { return ""; } // TS2741: 'validate' is missing
}

// Cause 4: Missing property in array of objects
const users: User[] = [
 { name: "Alice", age: 30 }, // TS2741: 'email' is missing
];

Solutions

Fix 1: Add all required properties

// Wrong
const user: User = { name: "Alice", age: 30 };

// Correct
const user: User = { name: "Alice", age: 30, email: "alice@example.com" };

Fix 2: Make optional properties with ?

// If email isn't always needed, mark it optional
interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
 email?: string; // Optional
}
const user: User = { name: "Alice", age: 30 }; // OK

Fix 3: Use default values or spread

function getConfig(): Config {
 const defaults = { debug: false };
 return { ...defaults, host: "localhost", port: 3000 };
}

Fix 4: Implement all interface members

class MyClass implements Serializable {
 serialize() { return ""; }
 validate() { return true; } // Implement missing method
}

Examples

// Example 1: React component props
interface ButtonProps {
 onClick: () => void;
 label: string;
 variant: "primary" | "secondary";
}
// Missing 'variant' prop
<Button onClick={handler} label="Click" />; // TS2741

// Example 2: API response type
interface ApiResponse {
 status: number;
 data: unknown;
 timestamp: string;
}
const res: ApiResponse = { status: 200, data: {} }; // TS2741

Related Errors

  • TS2322: Type is not assignable — broader assignability including extra properties.
  • TS2339: Property does not exist on type — accessing non-existent properties.
  • TS2749: Refers to a value, used as a type — value vs type confusion.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2749 — Refers to a Value But Used as a Type
item.description:
TS2749 — ‘X’ refers to a value, but is being used as a type here

TS2749 occurs when you use a value (like a class instance or variable) in a type position where TypeScript expects a type. This typically happens when importing a value instead of a type, or confusing class instances with class types.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Using a class instance as a type annotation
class User {
 name: string = "";
}
const user = new User();
type MyType = typeof user; // This is the instance type

let x: user; // TS2749: 'user' refers to a value, but is being used as a type

// Cause 2: Importing a value instead of a type
import MyComponent from "./MyComponent";
type Props = MyComponent; // TS2749 if MyComponent is a value

// Cause 3: Using a variable name as a type
const config = { host: "localhost" };
type T = config; // TS2749

// Cause 4: Forgetting to use 'typeof' for value-to-type conversion
function process(input: string) {}
const fn = process;
type FnType = fn; // TS2749

Solutions

Fix 1: Use ’typeof’ to convert a value to its type

class User {
 name: string = "";
}
const user = new User();

// Wrong
let x: user; // TS2749

// Correct
let x: typeof user; // OK — x has the same shape as user

// Better — use the class name directly
let x: User; // OK — this is the instance type

Fix 2: Import types using ‘import type’

// Wrong — imports the value
import MyComponent from "./MyComponent";

// Correct — imports only the type
import type MyComponent from "./MyComponent";
type Props = MyComponent; // OK

Fix 3: Use the class name as the type

class User {
 name: string = "";
}

// Wrong — use the class name, not an instance
const user = new User();
function process(u: user) {} // TS2749

// Correct
function process(u: User) {} // OK

Fix 4: Extract the type from a variable using typeof

const config = { host: "localhost", port: 3000 };

// Wrong
type T = config;

// Correct
type T = typeof config; // { host: string; port: number }

Examples

// Example 1: Common mistake with module exports
// utils.ts
export const helper = () => {};
export type HelperType = { name: string };

// app.ts
import { helper, HelperType } from "./utils";
type T = helper; // TS2749 — helper is a value
type T2 = HelperType; // OK — HelperType is a type

// Example 2: Using enum as a type
enum Color { Red, Green, Blue }
type T = Color; // TS2749 — Color is a value (enum)
type T2 = typeof Color; // OK

Related Errors

  • TS2304: Cannot find name — identifier not in scope.
  • TS2322: Type is not assignable — broader type issues.
  • TS2307: Cannot find module — module import issues.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2762 — JSX Element Missing Closing Tag
item.description:
TS2762 — JSX element ‘X’ has no corresponding closing tag

TS2762 occurs when a JSX element is opened but never closed. JSX requires all elements to either be self-closing (<Component />) or have a matching closing tag (</Component>).

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Missing closing tag
function App() {
 return <div>Hello; // TS2762: no closing </div>
}

// Cause 2: Missing self-closing slash
function App() {
 return <img src="photo.jpg">; // TS2762: should be <img ... />
}

// Cause 3: Mismatched tags
function App() {
 return (
 <div>
 <span>Hello</div> // TS2762: </span> missing
 </div>
 );
}

// Cause 4: Unclosed component
function App() {
 return <MyComponent props={value}; // TS2762: missing /> or </MyComponent>
}

Solutions

Fix 1: Add the closing tag

// Wrong
return <div>Hello;

// Correct
return <div>Hello</div>;

Fix 2: Use self-closing syntax for void elements

// Wrong
return <img src="photo.jpg">;

// Correct
return <img src="photo.jpg" />;

Fix 3: Ensure all nested tags are properly closed

// Wrong
return (
 <div>
 <span>Hello</div>
 </div>
);

// Correct
return (
 <div>
 <span>Hello</span>
 </div>
);

Fix 4: Close custom components properly

// Wrong
return <MyComponent props={value}>;

// Correct — self-closing
return <MyComponent props={value} />;

// Or with children
return (
 <MyComponent props={value}>
 <p>Child content</p>
 </MyComponent>
);

Examples

// Example 1: Conditional rendering without closing
function App({ show }: { show: boolean }) {
 return (
 <div>
 {show && <Alert message="Warning" // TS2762
 }
 </div>
 );
}

// Fix:
return (
 <div>
 {show && <Alert message="Warning" />}
 </div>
);

// Example 2: Fragment without closing
function App() {
 return (
 <>
 <p>First</p>
 <p>Second</p>
 // TS2762: missing </>
 );
}

Related Errors

  • TS2786: ‘X’ cannot be used as a JSX component — JSX component type issues.
  • TS1128: Declaration or statement expected — broader syntax error.
  • TS1434: Unexpected keyword or identifier — unexpected tokens.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2769 — No Overload Matches This Call
item.description:
TS2769 — No overload matches this call

TS2769 occurs when a function has multiple overload signatures but none of them match the arguments you provided. TypeScript checks each overload and if none are compatible, it reports this error.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Arguments don't match any overload
function process(value: string): void;
function process(value: number): void;
function process(value: boolean): void;
function process(value: any) {}

process({ key: "value" }); // TS2769: No overload matches this call

// Cause 2: Wrong argument types in overloaded function
function createElement(tag: "div"): HTMLDivElement;
function createElement(tag: "span"): HTMLSpanElement;
function createElement(tag: string): HTMLElement;
function createElement(tag: string) {}

createElement("canvas"); // TS2769: 'canvas' doesn't match specific overloads

// Cause 3: Missing arguments in some overloads
function fetch(url: string): Promise<Response>;
function fetch(url: string, init: RequestInit): Promise<Response>;
function fetch(url: string, init?: RequestInit) {}

fetch(); // TS2769: No overload matches

// Cause 4: Type mismatch with generic constraints
function merge<T extends object>(a: T, b: T): T & T {
 return { ...a, ...b };
}
merge("a", "b"); // TS2769: string is not an object

Solutions

Fix 1: Match one of the defined overload signatures

// Wrong
function process(value: string): void;
function process(value: number): void;
function process(value: any) {}
process({ key: "value" });

// Correct — use a valid type
process("hello"); // matches string overload
process(42); // matches number overload

Fix 2: Add a catch-all overload at the end

// Add an overload that accepts any type
function createElement(tag: "div"): HTMLDivElement;
function createElement(tag: "span"): HTMLSpanElement;
function createElement(tag: string): HTMLElement;
function createElement(tag: string) {} // catch-all

Fix 3: Use type assertions to satisfy an overload

const input = document.getElementById("canvas") as HTMLCanvasElement;

Fix 4: Restructure overloads to cover more cases

// Instead of specific overloads, use union types
function process(value: string | number | boolean) {}

Examples

// Example 1: React setState overload mismatch
interface State {
 count: number;
}
class Component {
 setState(state: Partial<State>) {}
}
this.setState({ count: "5" }); // TS2769

// Example 2: Array.reduce with wrong initial value
const nums = [1, 2, 3];
nums.reduce((acc, val) => acc + val); // TS2769 in some TS versions

Related Errors

  • TS2345: Argument type not assignable — no overloads involved, just a single signature.
  • TS2554: Expected X arguments, but got Y — wrong argument count.
  • TS2322: Type is not assignable — assignment rather than function call.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2786 — Cannot Be Used as a JSX Component
item.description:
TS2786 — ‘X’ cannot be used as a JSX component

TS2786 occurs when you use a value as a JSX component, but its type doesn’t match the expected JSX component interface. This commonly happens with React components that have incorrect prop types, missing type definitions, or when using third-party component libraries.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Function component with wrong return type
function MyComponent() {
 return null; // Returns null, which is valid JSX
}
// But if the function signature is wrong:
const MyComponent = () => 42; // TS2786: number is not a valid JSX element

// Cause 2: Using a non-component as a JSX element
const notAComponent = "hello";
return <notAComponent />; // TS2786

// Cause 3: Missing React types
// If @types/react is not installed
const App = () => <div>Hello</div>; // TS2786 if React types missing

// Cause 4: Class component not extending React.Component
class MyComponent {
 render() {
 return <div>Hello</div>; // TS2786: not a valid React component
 }
}

Solutions

Fix 1: Ensure the component returns a valid JSX element

// Wrong
const MyComponent = () => 42;

// Correct — return JSX, null, or a valid element
const MyComponent = () => <div>Hello</div>;
const MyComponent = () => null; // Valid in React

Fix 2: Install React type definitions

npm install --save-dev @types/react

Fix 3: Extend React.Component for class components

// Wrong
class MyComponent {
 render() {
 return <div>Hello</div>;
 }
}

// Correct
import React from "react";
class MyComponent extends React.Component {
 render() {
 return <div>Hello</div>;
 }
}

Fix 4: Use the correct function component signature

// Wrong — arrow function without proper typing
const MyComponent = (props) => <div>{props.name}</div>;

// Correct
interface Props {
 name: string;
}
const MyComponent: React.FC<Props> = ({ name }) => <div>{name}</div>;

Examples

// Example 1: Forwarded ref component
const MyComponent = React.forwardRef<HTMLDivElement>((props, ref) => {
 return <div ref={ref}>{props.children}</div>;
});
// If forwardRef types are wrong, TS2786 occurs

// Example 2: Higher-order component
function withAuth(Component: React.ComponentType) {
 return (props: any) => {
 if (!isLoggedIn()) return null;
 return <Component {...props} />; // TS2786 if ComponentType is wrong
 };
}

Related Errors

  • TS2762: JSX element missing closing tag — JSX syntax issues.
  • TS2322: Type is not assignable — prop type mismatches.
  • TS2339: Property does not exist on type — accessing non-existent props.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2820 — Action Is Not a Recognized Action
item.description:
TS2820 — Action ‘X’ is not a recognized action

TS2820 occurs when you use an unrecognized action property in a RequestInit or similar fetch-related configuration. The action property expects specific HTTP method strings, and TypeScript validates that the value is one of the recognized methods.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Typo in HTTP method
fetch("/api", { action: "get" }); // TS2820: 'get' is not a recognized action

// Cause 2: Using lowercase instead of uppercase
fetch("/api", { method: "get" }); // TS2820 if type expects uppercase

// Cause 3: Custom action string not in the union
fetch("/api", { action: "CUSTOM_ACTION" }); // TS2820

// Cause 4: Wrong property name
fetch("/api", { action: "GET" }); // TS2820 if 'action' isn't a valid property

Solutions

Fix 1: Use the correct HTTP method

// Wrong
fetch("/api", { action: "get" });

// Correct — use 'method' instead of 'action'
fetch("/api", { method: "GET" });

Fix 2: Use valid HTTP methods

// Wrong
fetch("/api", { method: "get" }); // lowercase

// Correct — uppercase
fetch("/api", { method: "GET" });

Fix 3: Use the correct RequestInit property

// Wrong
fetch("/api", { action: "POST" });

// Correct
fetch("/api", { method: "POST" });

Fix 4: Type the request options properly

const options: RequestInit = {
 method: "GET",
 headers: {
 "Content-Type": "application/json",
 },
};
fetch("/api", options);

Examples

// Example 1: Confusing 'action' with 'method'
fetch("/api/users", {
 action: "POST", // TS2820
 body: JSON.stringify({ name: "Alice" }),
});

// Fix:
fetch("/api/users", {
 method: "POST",
 body: JSON.stringify({ name: "Alice" }),
});

// Example 2: GraphQL fetch with custom options
fetch("/graphql", {
 method: "POST", // Not 'action'
 headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" },
 body: JSON.stringify({ query: "{ user { name } }" }),
});

Related Errors

  • TS2339: Property does not exist on type — using wrong property name.
  • TS2345: Argument type not assignable — wrong type in function args.
  • TS2322: Type is not assignable — type mismatch.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS7006 — Parameter Implicitly Has 'any' Type
item.description:
TS7006 — Parameter ‘X’ implicitly has an ‘any’ type

TS7006 occurs when noImplicitAny is enabled (part of strict mode) and a function parameter has no type annotation and TypeScript can’t infer its type. This forces you to explicitly declare types for all parameters.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Untyped function parameter
function add(a, b) { // TS7006: Parameter 'a' implicitly has an 'any' type
 return a + b;
}

// Cause 2: Callback without type annotations
[1, 2, 3].map(x => x * 2); // TS7006 if strict mode is on

// Cause 3: Event handler without type
document.addEventListener("click", (event) => { // TS7006: event implicitly has 'any'
 console.log(event.target);
});

// Cause 4: Destructured parameters without types
function process({ name, age }) { // TS7006
 console.log(name, age);
}

Solutions

Fix 1: Add explicit type annotations

// Wrong
function add(a, b) {
 return a + b;
}

// Correct
function add(a: number, b: number): number {
 return a + b;
}

Fix 2: Type callback parameters

// Wrong
[1, 2, 3].map(x => x * 2);

// Correct — TypeScript can usually infer this from the array type
// But if needed:
[1, 2, 3].map((x: number) => x * 2);

Fix 3: Use typed DOM event handlers

// Wrong
document.addEventListener("click", (event) => {});

// Correct
document.addEventListener("click", (event: MouseEvent) => {});

Fix 4: Type destructured parameters

// Wrong
function process({ name, age }) {}

// Correct
interface UserData {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}
function process({ name, age }: UserData) {}

Examples

// Example 1: Arrow function in array method
const doubled = [1, 2, 3].map(n => n * 2); // TS7006: 'n' implicitly has 'any'

// Example 2: Event listener
button.addEventListener("click", e => {
 e.target; // TS7006
});

// Fix: button.addEventListener("click", (e: MouseEvent) => { ... });

Related Errors

  • TS7031: Binding element implicitly has ‘any’ type — destructured parameters.
  • TS2304: Cannot find name — type not imported.
  • TS18046: ‘X’ is of type ‘unknown’ — strict typing with unknown.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS7031 — Binding Element Implicitly Has 'any' Type
item.description:
TS7031 — Binding element ‘X’ implicitly has an ‘any’ type

TS7031 occurs when destructured parameters in a function don’t have type annotations and noImplicitAny is enabled. This is similar to TS7006 but specifically targets destructured binding elements.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Destructured function parameter without types
function greet({ name, age }) { // TS7031: 'name' implicitly has 'any' type
 return `Hello ${name}, you are ${age}`;
}

// Cause 2: Destructured in arrow functions
const process = ({ id, label }) => { // TS7031
 console.log(id, label);
};

// Cause 3: Destructured in method parameters
class Service {
 handle({ data, status }) { // TS7031
 return data;
 }
}

// Cause 4: Destructured with defaults but no type
function configure({ host = "localhost", port = 3000 }) { // TS7031
 console.log(host, port);
}

Solutions

Fix 1: Annotate the destructured parameter with an interface

interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}

// Wrong
function greet({ name, age }) {}

// Correct
function greet({ name, age }: User) {}

Fix 2: Use inline type annotations

// Wrong
function greet({ name, age }) {}

// Correct
function greet({ name, age }: { name: string; age: number }) {}

Fix 3: Type destructured defaults

// Wrong
function configure({ host = "localhost", port = 3000 }) {}

// Correct
function configure({ host = "localhost", port = 3000 }: { host?: string; port?: number }) {}

Fix 4: Type arrow function parameters

// Wrong
const process = ({ id, label }) => {};

// Correct
const process = ({ id, label }: { id: number; label: string }) => {};

Examples

// Example 1: React component props
function Button({ onClick, label, disabled }) { // TS7031
 return <button onClick={onClick} disabled={disabled}>{label}</button>;
}

// Fix: type the props
interface ButtonProps {
 onClick: () => void;
 label: string;
 disabled?: boolean;
}
function Button({ onClick, label, disabled }: ButtonProps) {}

Related Errors

  • TS7006: Parameter implicitly has ‘any’ type — non-destructured parameters.
  • TS2339: Property does not exist on type — related to object shape.
  • TS2741: Property is missing in type — missing required properties.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript Type 'never' Has No Properties — Exhaustive Check Fix
item.description:
TypeScript: Type ’never’ has no properties

The never type represents values that never occur — a function that always throws, or a variable that can never be assigned a value. When you see “Type ’never’ has no properties”, you are trying to access properties or call methods on a value that TypeScript has determined is unreachable. This is actually a powerful feature for exhaustive type checking.

Common Causes

  • Switch statement missing a case — TypeScript narrows to never when all cases are covered, but if you add a new case, the never check catches it
  • Dead code after return/throw — code that TypeScript knows cannot execute
  • Incorrect type narrowing — a condition that TypeScript determines is impossible
  • Generic default resolving to never — a type parameter that defaults to never is used directly

How to Fix

// Cause 1: Switch statement not exhaustive
type Shape = "circle" | "square" | "triangle";

function area(shape: Shape): number {
 switch (shape) {
 case "circle":
 return 3.14;
 case "square":
 return 1;
 // Missing "triangle" case — no error here but adding it later causes issues
 }
}

// Fix: use exhaustive check
function area(shape: Shape): number {
 switch (shape) {
 case "circle":
 return 3.14;
 case "square":
 return 1;
 case "triangle":
 return 0.5;
 default:
 const _exhaustive: never = shape; // compile error if case is missing
 return _exhaustive;
 }
}

// Cause 2: Accessing property on narrowed never
function process(value: string | number) {
 if (typeof value === "string" && typeof value === "number") {
 // This condition is impossible — value is 'never' here
 console.log(value.toUpperCase()); // TS2339: 'never' has no property 'toUpperCase'
 }
}

// Cause 3: Return type is never
function throwError(msg: string): never {
 throw new Error(msg);
}

const result = throwError("oops");
result.foo; // TS2339: 'never' has no property 'foo'

Examples

// Example 1: Exhaustive discriminated union check
interface Circle { kind: "circle"; radius: number; }
interface Square { kind: "square"; side: number; }
type Shape = Circle | Square;

function describe(shape: Shape): string {
 switch (shape.kind) {
 case "circle":
 return `Circle with radius ${shape.radius}`;
 case "square":
 return `Square with side ${shape.side}`;
 default:
 const _check: never = shape;
 return _check; // ensures all cases handled
 }
}

// Example 2: never in conditional types
type NonNullable<T> = T extends null | undefined ? never : T;
type A = NonNullable<string | null>; // string

// Example 3: Asserting never for impossible paths
function processInput(input: unknown): string {
 if (typeof input === "string") return input;
 if (typeof input === "number") return String(input);
 if (typeof input === "boolean") return input ? "yes" : "no";
 const _impossible: never = input; // input is never — all cases handled
 return _impossible;
}

Related Errors

  • TS2322: Type not assignable — type narrowing leads to never
  • TS2345: Argument type not assignable — never passed where concrete type expected
  • TS2578: Unused ‘@ts-expect-error’ directive — related to unreachable code

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript Type Assignment Error — Type Not Assignable to Type Fix
item.description:
TypeScript: Type ‘X’ is not assignable to type ‘Y’

This error occurs when you assign a value of one type to a variable, parameter, or return position that expects a different, incompatible type. TypeScript’s structural type system checks that the source type satisfies all requirements of the target type.

Common Causes

  • Primitive type mismatch — assigning a string to a number variable
  • Missing required properties — an object literal is missing fields required by the target interface
  • Strict null violations — assigning null or undefined to a non-nullable type (with strictNullChecks)
  • Enum or literal type mismatch — using a value from one enum where another is expected

How to Fix

// Cause 1: Primitive mismatch
let age: number = "twenty-five"; // TS2322

// Fix: use the correct type
let age: number = 25;

// Cause 2: Missing properties
interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}
const user: User = { name: "Alice" }; // TS2322

// Fix: provide all required fields
const user: User = { name: "Alice", age: 30 };

// Cause 3: Null assigned to non-nullable
let name: string = null; // TS2322

// Fix: use union type
let name: string | null = null;

// Cause 4: Wrong function return type
function getLength(): number {
 return "hello"; // TS2322
}

// Fix: return the correct type
function getLength(): string {
 return "hello";
}

Examples

// Enum mismatch
enum Color { Red, Green, Blue }
enum Direction { Up, Down }
let c: Color = Color.Red;
let d: Direction = c; // TS2322: Color not assignable to Direction

// Excess properties in object literal
interface Config {
 host: string;
 port: number;
}
const config: Config = { host: "localhost", port: 3000, debug: true }; // TS2322

// Function argument mismatch
function greet(name: string) {}
greet(42); // TS2322: number not assignable to string

Related Errors

  • TS2345: Argument of type not assignable — similar but for function arguments
  • TS2741: Property is missing in type — missing required properties
  • TS2352: Conversion may be a mistake — type assertion issues
